text_content: Just so everyone is aware, yes cougars/pumas/mountain lions can be owned in the in most US states with a permit (some don't require one). And yes by definition cougars are not considered big cats (like lion and tigers) because they are lacking the hyoid bone adaptation that allows them to roar (and some other bone structure that are different). But this does not make them ok to own. They are still wild animals that are incredibly strong and can weigh 75-160 lbs. they don't typically attack humans but there have been cases of them killing people. Cougars typically have a territory size of 50-150 sq miles which would be hard to provide in a suburban home. Let's not normalize keeping large predatory animals as pets

text_content: When I was a child, I lived with my aunt and uncle, and she would feed this cat that was hidden under a office trailer in the middle of an abandoned car dealership. Edit: Thanks for the love to those who liked, but this will be my one and only edit because apparently I have to be a science teacher to get the... "not so intelligent" people out of my replies, so here it goes. 
A panther is not an animal. 
The term "panther" is a genus, or class of animals, describing big cats. A "Black panther" is not a specific animal. It's a member of the panthera genus with melanism. Melanism makes you black, hence the name "Black panther". 
It's a description, not an animal. A black lion is a black panther, a black tiger is a black panther, in the same way that a black jaguar is a black panther. video_description: Some say the lion is king... but the jaguar hunts in silence, crushes skulls, and swims like a pro. With the strongest bite of any big ...
text_content: My one story about jaguars:  I was at a nature park in Southern Mexico, and came upon an enclosure labeled "jaguar."  It was a large enclosure with the natural, thick, local limestone rainforest habitat, but there wasn't any part of the enclosure that was blocked from view.  I looked and looked but couldn't see any jaguars in there, so I figured I'd had the misfortune to come when the enclosure was empty for cleaning, maintenance, etc.  I was just about to walk away when the black jaguar who had been lounging on a tree limb stretched, yawned, and repositioned itself.  It was literally right in front of me, not 20 feet away, and I had been looking straight at it for at least a minute.  But it blended so well with the broken sunlight and random shadow pattern of the jungle canopy that I hadn't been able to see it until it moved--it had been completely invisible to my eyes and brain, even though I had been actively looking for it.  If we'd have been in the bush, I could have walked right into that enormous murder cat and never have seen it, and obviously, it could have killed me before I had any idea I was even in any danger.  It was an amazing demonstration of how effective their camouflage is in their natural environment, and a sobering demonstration of something a local jungle guide told me:  if you walk into jaguar territory and walk out alive, it's because the jaguar decided to allow you to live that day.

text_content: What Jon says here confirms what I have heard from other sources.  Setting aside the problem of how to ethically purchase a healthy cheetah cub, the biggest challenge with maintaining a healthy cheetah in captivity is properly sourcing their diet.  If cheetahs do not receive the proper mix of proteins and other nutrients, they will experience various chronic health problems and experience shortened life spans.  From a personality and instinct perspective, cheetahs are the easiest big cat to adapt to a human environment.  Far easier in fact than tigers, lions, leopards, jaguars or pumas, who are potentially very dangerous even when hand-raised from infancy by their humans.  If socialized from cubs to interact with humans, cheetahs will bond and imprint on their human family, and interact like an overgrown house cat.  That said, they instinctually want to mark their territory -- and a 75- to 125-pount cheetah can produce a lot of urine.  They also want to claw and chew inanimate objects.  These are not "indoor" cats; they need to be kept outside a human home, with access to appropriate open space for exercise and play.  At best, having a pet cheetah is an expensive luxury that requires a significant input of cash, time and effort that is beyond the reach of most human beings to provide.

video_title: This Cheetah Thinks It’s a Pet Cat. #animal #cute
text_content:
No, cheetahs don't "know that they'd lose that fight". A cheetah could definitely kill a human. Those who think cheetahs are weak, fail to realize how much force is required to accelerate to 70 mph faster than an F-1 car. Cheetahs can accomplish that astonishing feat of strength, because the muscles needed to leap forward are over-sized and over-charged with fast twitch muscles, and every other part of the body is as lightweight as possible.

The reasons why a cheetah would lose against a heavier cat or hyena are that 1) they have more muscles in total, and 2) their more balanced muscle distribution allows multiple fighting techniques.

Against a human, though, none of that would apply. Simply grabbing us and kicking us in the gut would result in complete disembowelment in a fraction of a second. Our last sight would be our guts airborne above the savannah (exaggeration for effect).

Fortunately for us, cheetah instincts are adapted to deal with other animals, where the rule is that slow animals have good and dangerous defenses, while fast animals don't. Cheetah specialization is to avoid dealing with defenses, and instead dealing with flight. Quite simply, the fact that humans are pathetically slow is a major reason why they don't see us as prey. Another reason is that cheetahs are solitary. Any serious injury is a death sentence, even with a technical "victory". If a creature isn't known to be safe prey, a cheetah won't risk it.

text_content:
Fun fact: unlike Lions, Tigers...and pretty much any other large-ish predatory mammal, Cheetah do not get the compulsory instinct to attack a human when their back is turned. They're more likely to start rubbing against you if they feel comfortable enough in your presence, or simply keep their distance if not.

Out of all known African predators, Cheetahs are probably the most un-suited for the environment, as they're truly a glass-cannon build: they can dish out decent enough damage, but absolutely fold under even remotely similar responses. They cannot protect their kills, and cheetah cubs are considered easy targets as the parents (knowing the innate fragility of cheetah existence) will struggle to put up much of a fight if forced to protect them.

Cheetahs are one of the few species that was already being out-competed by other predators before humans arrived on the scene and made things worse.

text_content: I recommend gentle dominance with cats.  Most felines don’t comprehend punishment very well and usually will listen out of fear or be anti-social.  If a cat is being persistently hazardous and/ or destructive, I usually pick them up by supporting under the chest with one hand and gently picking them up by the scruff of their neck and relocating.  I try to use physical behavior only when necessary though and calling their name or yelling is usually the first resort.  I’ve met a lot of very aggressive animals in my life who were abused but if you give them space and occasional treats they’ll usually open up to you on their own, just don’t rush it and move slow and in sight if it’s a particularly antisocial animal.  Be calm and relaxed but also prepared to pull away if any defensive aggression is displayed towards you.  Dominance discipline is usually not needed because the right behavior will display dominance.  When you provide food, set boundaries, and occupy your territory, a smaller animal can usually sense you’re the boss unless you act too enabling

text_content:
I used to have a bear cub. Yea sounds crazy, and it was crazy. We never kept him in the house, he was free to go anywhere whenever he pleased. 
He came to our door everynight, and stayed near our house to visit us most times we were outside. We assumed his mother had died because we never saw her. So we fed him. It got the the point where we would pet him and play with him like he was a dog. 
Months pass and he had become family to us. After a few days of not seeing him, we found out he had been shot and killed through facebook.
The man who killed him saw him come near our house and thought he was like any other bear. He wasnt a pet. He was a medium size male black bear. 
I dont remember what we used to call him. I was a kid so I know I had to have given him a name at some point. All I remember is that he LOVED honey buns.
Wild animals can be tamed, not domesticated - and nobody should take them from their natural environment. Even small friendly interactions can impact their wellbeing. ❤❤

text_content:
I had an F2 Savannah. Lovely boy. Very good guard dog too. He was huge and quite beefy. Like a beefy greyhound. A box of 5 of them was dumped on my driveway. I think they thought it was a mis-mating, sadly a lot of dodgy breeders where we lived.

I'd already climbed about 10 foot up the side of a cliff to recuse two kittens someone had thrown off the top. Tide was out at the time. They were bengals with belly button hernias. You're not supposed to continue breeding from a queen if you see this happen as it's a hereditary condition. But like I said dodgy breeders!!! So being the countryside and everyone knowing everyone, I must be insane. I just had to make sure they didn't strangle the hernias and both girls were neutered on a midline incision rather than on the side and hernia popped back in. Then rehomed
one of them.  

 Well I took the box of kittens to the vet to check they were okay. She had the genetic markers tested and it must have been a bengal that got at the Queen as these were F2 savannahs. 

We then had to call everyone who we'd rehired a kitten to and tell them to insure their cats for 10k and don't let them outside on their own if they were friendly. They also had to have them neutered.

I kept the cheekiest. He was happy as heck with us but totally German Shepard with those he didn't know. He loved going out in the forest with us on a harness and lead and we lived down a long drive so there were three huge paddocks and he'd be okay out running around those. He was also trained on command to bring his pompoms for playing fetch.

I did have to explain to a friend that he didn't know her and she was not going to enjoy it when she kept trying to play with him. And he did attack her. Bengals are bred from wild cats and cervals are wild cats so you know he had German Shepard sized paws but razor sharp. 

He was great with the other cats though. My bengal girl decided she was his mum, well for the few weeks she was bigger than him, but she still let her clean him. He also would want to know everything I was doing, including when cooking. 

And if he felt a kid was getting to close to my child he was big enough to put that child to the ground. My child would say don't move, he wouldn't hurt them, and I'd then call him and he'd go back to happy cat again and come to me. 

When we went out for walks he wasn't territorial and would let strangers pet him. Postman was scare sh*tless of him though!

video_title: Would a Capuchin Monkey Make a Good Pet? | Pets Rated 😲 #capuchinmonkey #animals
video_description: Smart, curious, and incredibly social—capuchin monkeys can be adorable and challenging at the same time. Could they make a ...
text_content:
Yep. You probably need to know the difference between a Capuchin and Macaque monkeys first. Then try and speak from a place of authority and expertise. Not knowing the simple difference in two breeds of monkeys probably hurts the message that I believe you are trying to get across. Monkeys are an amazing little life form. Their eyes, faces, little human like hands, and curious playful nature are hard to resist. Their similarity to humans is amazing and why so many people enjoy watching them. The fact we find them so adorable and want to hug and take care of all of them is something programmed deep within our being. Since similar to humans, both male and female humans feel the way they do about them because babies propagate and ensure the survival of a species. The will of the cells to multiply and copy themselves drives all living things. However, that cannot occur if the babies don't live and survive long enough to repeat the once again. So, monkey babies being similar in appearance to that of human babies, subconsciously drives us to protect, provide, and comfort them. The owning of a primate, great or small, as a pet isn't the best for the monkeys. These little beings were designed by the creator to florish in their natural habitats. Despite the fact humans are encroaching into the monkeys home, more on that in a bit, they still belong in their home. As someone who rescues and rehabilitates primates whenever that very thing occurs, most of our rescues don't come from a situation of the little guys being injuried from saws or bulldozers. Instead they came from uniformed and in most cases good intentioned people trying to make them their pet.
 Typically, they will have everything they think a monkey needs. A very large enclosure, the right foods, toys, treats, and lots of love. However, even that is not enough to make sure these wonderful beings have the opportunity to live long fulfilling monkey lives. With baby monkeys, depending on their age and breed, have very strenuous feeding cycles. A capuchin monkey around 2 weeks old needs to be fed every 2 to 3 hours, day and night. At this age, they are usually fed a specialized milk replacer or formula via a small 1cc syringe to prevent aspiration. Thats day and night. Most people struggle with that sort of a feeding cycle with a human baby let alone a fury one. This must be followed up with many monkey babies by having to rub their genital area with a warm, damp tissue after every feeding to stimulate urination and defecation. Wow, not something people are accustomed too. Then the baby capuchin must also be kept in an incubator or at a consistent warm temperature (85°F or above) as they cannot regulate their own body temperature at this age. Just to name a few. 

 Whenever people get frustrated, busy, have a human baby, lose a job, or suffer one of a litany of possible lifestyle changes such as a drastic income change or they can no longer dedicate the amount of time needed to give monkeys the attention or care they need for a healthy life. The other big one we run into is that people aren't prepared for is when the cute babies turn into older monkeys. Sometimes a monkey can get aggressive at varying times in their lives including hormone changes and that desire to mate. This isn't the monkeys fault because this is how they are designed deep in their DNA. That isn't something you should punish as this is completely natural. They have similar emotions and feelings just like we do and can get mad, depressed, stressed out and can even get very possessive with their owner. Whenever they get jealous or possessive those little human like arms, hands, and fingers become poop flinging, fist-pounding, object throwing expressions of those feelings. Thats when they either get mistreated, abandoned, and neglected. 

Regardless of the reason their owners no longer can keep them and places like the one a work at come into play. Although their owners may have started out with good intentions, spent untold fortunes on an enclosure as big as some people's entire house, provided vet care, love them, and will end up missing them just like anyone would whenever they say goodbye to a family member. The results are the same. The monkey suffers and doesn't get the opportunity to live like they would if they were left alone in their natural habitat. Thats when organizations and sanctuaries get involved. Sadly, there are far too many stories I could share about what our little fury buddies must have went through. Some of them had been living in the most disgusting and vile environments you could imagine. The most heartbreaking thing of all wasn't just the abhorrent state of their physical health but their mental state as well. Abandon, mistreated, and in the care of total strangers these monkeys have had their hearts crushed. All they know is how to be a monkey and a few party tricks they have been taught to put on shows for money. They don't fully understand the minds of mankind. Many times their owners have had them since they were babies. All they know is you! Now, for at least the second time in their life, that pillar of stability, security, and  consistency is gone. They again have lost both things suddenly and they think it was them that caused this. Please, use this data to make an informed decision.

video_title: Would You Own a Capuchin Monkey 😱🐒 (Cutest Wild Pet Ever!)
video_description: Ever dreamed of owning a monkey? Meet the Capuchin – the energetic, adorable, and super smart primate that could change ...
text_content:
A monkey of any description is a very bad choice as a pet. They are a 30-40 year commitment that requires a lot of attention and special treatment. They urinate and defecate wherever and whenever they feel the urge. Mature females menstruate, and mature males masturbate. They will throw feces if they become angry. They stink and are very messy. They require special veterinary care and a very specific diet. They require a lot of mental stimulation that places a huge demand on the owner. When they are babies, they are sweet, docile and adorable. But when they mature, they can become aggressive and even dangerous. 

Monkeys are social animals that belong in troops. They must have this social interaction in order to be mentally healthy. They are not "live toys" that owners force to walk upright, wear doll clothes, and perform tricks. Monkeys need to be left in their natural habitat and not be kept prisoner by creatures (humans) with which they ultimately are not compatible.

text_content: I can believe Jason got arrested. A person who lived two houses down from me had a wolf dog hybrid. He had frequently broken state laws regarding enclosures for wolf dogs. The dog almost attacked me once. It was killing small animals including a neighbor's cat. Then one night another neighbors sister and brother-in-law were leaving their house, and somehow their car keys had fallen out of a jacket. The sister-in-law was holding their baby by the car door waiting for her husband to find the keys when they heard a growl. The wolf dog had her cornered and she couldn't get in the car. My neighbor fired two shots in the air, then one close to the wolf dog, but it never backed away so finally he felt he had no choice but to shoot it. He was charged, but he reached a settlement with the owner of the wolf dog and courts where he had to pay for medical bills, and court costs.  He was facing a felony, but he was a bailiff and if he didn't take the deal, he would have most likely lost his job.  This was even though it was found out the dogs owner had left the dog there for a week while he went to visit his girlfriend, but just left a big pile of food and a bucket of water in the enclosure. The dog probably ate all the food within the first couple of days and was starving. Nobody blamed the dog, including the neighbor who shot him. We all blamed the dogs owner plus whoever was in charge of fines or assessing those fines to the owner when he had repeatedly broken the law.  That's the biggest issue in my opinion. The laws and enforcement of laws regarding all types of animals, not just wild animals, are too lacks.  And usually it is an innocent victim and the animal who pays the price, and rarely the person who owns the animal.

text_content: In the UK, from what I remember from zoo management classes, is that a chimp is classified as a class 1 dangerous animal. That means staff in a zoo or collection still have a chance of recapturing the animal while on the premises as long as its not causing a threat to human life, but once it escapes from the zoo entirely, the chances are it will just be killed. Further recapture may be tried if the animal has not gotten near residential area, but the moment it gets too close to residential areas, the risk of harm outweighs the life of the animal. Like in this case, tranquillisers are not instant, and are made less effective by adrenaline. Jason had every right.

text_content:
I worked at a petting zoo that had some lemurs. They were *mean*. I was bit the one and only time I went into an enclosure to clean it. It was a small nip, basically equivalent to a cat scratch, but it wasn't casually brushed off, as I stopped working for the day and got it bandaged up.

I wasn't allowed to get close to the area where they had 3 aggressive lemurs (I cleaned an enclosure with only one, much calmer overall lemur), and all the enclosures had basically two doors on them, with a little area in between, and you always kept at least one door closed at all times.

They also had much more going on in their areas, with toys and whatnot, and they were routinely rotated throughout 3 different enclosures so they had more variety in their life. All this was for lemurs, who are both much smaller add far less dangerous than chimpanzees, but were very clearly treated much better and held to a higher safety standard by this small business rentable petting zoo than anyone in this video working with large, dangerous, understimulated great apes.

video_title: Pet Chimp Eats Man! #joerogan #storytime #chimpanzee
text_content:
The story Joe is referring to is St. James Davis & his wife LaDonna Davis and their chimpanzee Moe.

After Moe had bit several people in West Covina, California, the chimp was relocated to an animal sanctuary. 

When the two came to visit, they brought birthday cake, toys, chocolate, and even candy hearts. They were seated at a picnic table celebrating his birthday when two adult male chimps, Buddy & Ollie rushed the wife and bit her thumb off. St. James pushed his wife underneath the table to protect her and the chimps began attacking him simultaneously. They attacked his face including his nose and lips, his left foot, destroyed most of his fingers, ripped off his testicles, his buttocks, part of his torso. The sanctuary’s owners son-in-law retrieved a .45 pistol and shot the chimp Buddy in the head. Ollie dragged St. James body down an empty corridor where the son-in-law then killed him by gunshot. Their chimp Moe was not involved in the attack, but it’s unclear if he tried to save or protect either of them. 

St. James spent six months in the hospital recovering, some of which he was in a coma. He underwent 60 surgeries, has a glass eye, and two slits where his nose used to be.

Interestingly enough, in 2008 the Davises chimp Moe escaped from his sanctuary. There have been reports of sightings near homes where he was last housed, as well as claims he was heading toward a mountain. The Davises hired a helicopter to do flyovers of the San Bernardino National Forest in an attempt to find him… but to date the 42-year old chimp has never been found. 

Thank you Wikipedia.

video_title: Owning a Pet Lemur Myths | True or False!
video_description: Myths and claims about pet lemurs, do they make bad pets? Do they become aggressive to their owners? Are they illegal?
text_content:
I have been reading about lemurs for months now but not out of a will to own them. They are endangered animals and often critically so. They also are not like some house cat that you can easily keep. Lemurs have their own particular way of life which does not include humans. They belong into the wild or at least sanctuaries where professional conservators can care and support them in their way of life. The Duke Lemur Center would be a good example for exactly such an effort. And unsurpringly, they also speak out against owning one as a pet just as anyone who actually works with lemurs in conservation, studies, field work or something else in academia.

video_description: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? As one can imagine, it's easy to fall for their cute faces ...
text_content:
No.  Monkeys - in general - do NOT make "good" pets.

Unless you are very well-prepared and aware of what monkeys are and do, don't get a monkey. They are too intelligent, curious, and possessive to be a good room mate. They will shit and piss anywhere, and at any time.  You cannot physically discipline a monkey, nor is it easy to do so by restricting them from activity or out of cage freedoms. They will bear a grudge. They will bite if you attempt to lift it off the couch, and throw their shit at you as you pass the cage.

This is why they end up mean and vicious, and needing rescue.

DON'T get a monkey.

Their biggest threat is the pet trade but this video almost sounds like a commercial for getting one as a pet.

So if we are cutting down trees, it would have to stop exactly where the last tree fell. If we need more trees than we need to convert some other land that is already in use by humans. The relinquished land back to nature must be in great condition and all traces of human contamination should have be cleaned up and removed.

I don’t like the way they move their heads so fast, stops before I would expect it to, and they constantly move their head and in so many different directions. They could just have moved their eyes instead. It makes them like shifty. Plus I was a little grossed out by the way they eat tree sap. No need for stretching the sap back and forth, thin to thick. Their twitchy little critters.

Although the actions depicted in this video are viewed as harmless, animal experts assert that these animals experience high levels of stress from being tortured during the course of such "performances": Dr. Anna Nekaris, an anthropologist who specializes in slow-loris research at Oxford Brookes University states that the loris willingness to be held is not because they are comfortable but rather it is part of their defense mechanism — to be still and silent when they feel threatened. The only reason the loris isn't biting the person holding it in the video is because it has had its teeth cruelly ripped out by illegal traders who poach them from their natural rain forest habitat. The teeth are removed because the loris can deliver a toxic bite. The reason the tickled slow loris is seen raising its arms (a sign of distress) is because it is making an attempt to defend itself by reaching its toxic gland: The slow loris is the world's only poisonous primate. Its venom is stored in an elbow patch. When it is feeling threatened, the slow loris will raise its arms above its head in a diamond shape, suck in the poison from its elbow patch, then mix it around in its mouth before delivering the toxic bite. These animals are also often in pain because they are nocturnal and not equipped to deal with extended periods of daylight. In addition to causing a high level of stress in the animal, this video also encourages the illegal trade of slow lorises: The popularity of slow loris videos has led to an increase in the number of people wanting to have a slow loris as a pet. And while it may appear cute, the animal in the video is suffering and so is the entire species. Slow lorises are shoved into plastic 
crates and bags; their teeth are clipped out with pliers or wire cutters with no anesthesia, no aftercare, no medicine and most die; multiple animals are crammed into tiny cages where they wound each other with their venomous bites. Large percentages of captured slow lorises die due to inhumane, overcrowded, stressful conditions during transportation often with the bodies of others that have died on the journey. This painful process of mutilation not only often kills the animals via blood loss or infection, but also prevents rescued lorises from being released back into the wild. Not only does the slow loris pet trade cause unimaginable suffering, it is also the biggest threat to the survival of the species, which is in serious danger of extinction. All eight species of slow lorises are considered “threatened” and the Javan slow loris is one of the 25 most endangered primates worldwide. So if you have a heart, please do not like or share this video.

text_content: Because of their "cuteness", videos of pet slow lorises are some of the mostly frequently watched animal-related viral videos on YouTube.[60][123] In March 2011, a newly posted video of a slow loris holding a cocktail umbrella had been viewed more than two million times, while an older video of a slow loris being tickled had been viewed more than six million times.[131] According to Nekaris, these videos are misunderstood by most people who watch them, since most do not realize that it is illegal in most countries to own them as pets and that the slow lorises in the videos are only docile because that is their passive defensive reaction to threatening situations.[123][131] Despite frequent advertisements by pet shops in Japan, the World Conservation Monitoring Centre reported only a few dozen slow lorises were imported in 2006, suggesting frequent smuggling.[83] Slow lorises are also smuggled to China, Taiwan, Europe, Russia, the United States, and Saudi Arabia for use as pets.[130][120][131]

text_content:
Slow loris
"Their huge round eyes, adorable faces and almost human-like paws have made slow lorises one of the internet's favourite animals. Being nocturnal, the little primates seem almost docile during the day: when they do move around, they do so slowly and deliberately. When threatened, they tend to freeze.
But slow lorises also have a lethal ace up their sleeve, almost literally.
In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death
When threatened, a loris will often raise its arms above its head. Far from being a sign of surrender, this is a defensive posture, bringing a gland around its elbow close to its mouth.
By combining the oil secreted from the gland with saliva, the loris creates a strong venom, which it then smears onto the top of its head, making it a toxic morsel for any predator.
The loris can also keep the venom inside its mouth, delivering it in a bite. The incisors at the front of the bottom jaw help to conduct the liquid upwards, ensuring it gets into any wound it can inflict.
According to Anne-Isola Nekaris of Oxford Brookes University, a bite from a slow loris is "intensely painful". "In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death," Nekaris says.
There are nine known species of slow loris living in the forests of South East Asia. The venom of the pygmy slow loris (Nycticebus pygmaeus) has been found to contain 220 compounds.
So, the next time you watch a video of a slow loris being "tickled" with its arms in the air, remember it is not enjoying the experience: it is waiting for a chance to strike."

video_title: Do SQUIRREL MONKEYS make good PETS?
video_description: Who hasn't dreamt about having a pet monkey? In this video, we look into what it actually takes to have a squirrel monkey as a pet ...
text_content: Knew a family who had a tiny squirrel monkey for almost 30 years.  He slept in his cage but knew how to open the door so essentially ran free in their house.   He was housetrained to use his cage which was lined with newspaper but preferred the cats' litterboxes (they also had up to 3 cats).  The cats initially tried stalking him because he was tiny but he quickly demonstrated that he could put some serious hurt on them if he wanted to.   He could be destructive, and I wouldn't recommend one as a pet to anyone else.  But they had a large family with a lot of kids around to play with him and keep an eye on him.  They had to keep their cupboards locked because he knew how to open them and would tear open cereal boxes and smash peanut butter and jelly jars on the floor if given the opportunity.  He had astonishing speed, leaping ability, and coordination and so 'won' almost any game of tag or keep-away he played with the kids.  He escaped the house a few times and was un-catchable.  During his escapes, he lived in the trees and ate insects and raided the neighbors' bird feeders and any pet food they left outside, but would be back tapping at a window on the first cold night.

video_title: Baby monkey realises he is not with his foster mom
video_description: Orphan baby monkey Iidiko realises Crumpet is not his true foster mom and jumps into hazels arms The Vervet Forest is a wildlife ...
text_content:
Non-human primates are amazing animals to watch. There are a lot of raw emotions and foster moms, whose hormones, and watching other females claim foster babies, and instincts help them out a lot too, when it comes to their turn to become a “mum” also! 

What also really amazes me are the similarities to humans, in their behaviour and their maternal instincts. If you look at sheep and other livestock and other mammals around the world, in order for a farmer or rescuer and/or caregiver to convince moms whose babies have been stillborn or don’t make it long past birth, they usually rub the birthing and afterbirth fluids from the dead baby onto the orphaned or rejected baby and that is how to facilitate an adoption amongst those animals! 

Monkeys and apes are smarter than that. So it just goes to show that they can be empathetic, have a maternal drive for a baby not belonging to them, by choice and not because they were tricked into it!

And here is the proof as to how the adoption process pan out. When this foster mom took this orphan on, he/she was more than happy to call her mom in every way that was important. So much so, that even unknowingly sitting with another troop member (I’m guessing female as a lot of, probably most monkeys that raise their young communally in a troop, will have aunts and sisters of the infant babysit from time to time, it’s also how sisters of the baby learn skills to become a mother of their own one day!), when the infant realised it, that screech of concern and leap into her mother’s arms is exactly how a baby born to a female would act in that situation! Mom opened her arms and baby leapt into them and only when clinging to mom and embraced by her; does the baby peek out again to see what’s going on ❤

video_title: Bush Baby: The Pet Everyone Wants
video_description: Bush babies are popular on social media but can they be pets? More Information: ...
text_content:
Bushbaby as pets?
The tiny "Nagapie" (night monkey in Afrikaans) are at risk due to humans wanting to keep them as inappropriate pets. 
 
Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

text_content:
Bushbaby as pets?
The tiny "Nagapie" (night monkey in Afrikaans) are at risk due to humans wanting to keep them as inappropriate pets. 
 
Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

text_content:
I have a 13 year old male CAG. He has been “my” bird up until just recently.  He’s now taken a shine to my daughter.  😊 which is fine with me. Because even though I was his person, he’d bite me all the time. And, I’m not talking love bites. He bit for blood. I have so many scars!  

These birds are so smart! They really shouldn’t be pets.  They belong out in the wild to be free to fly and be birds.  I also have a Senegal parrot.  He’s a very clingy bird that just wants to be with me all the time. He’s smart as well.  But, I think all parrots are a little psycho, they aren’t completely domesticated.  They are still wild and deserve to be free. Again, they deserve to be free to fly.  I think that’s why people give up parrots. They don’t realize the work that parrots are and they don’t want to deal with them.  Or, sadder still, they keep them in dark rooms, feed them and forget them.  

No, mine will never be let free.  I’ll make sure they are taken care of, even after I’m gone.  If you feel you must get a parrot, please, please adopt a bird.  Please do not purchase one.  If people adopt, it would send a message to the bird breeders and the stores.

text_content: I had one for a little while but it never talked so I traded it with someone who had a double yellow head Amazon (and that bird talked up a storm).  What I will say what I loved about the African Gray is they are not hard biters while the Amazon can deliver a vicious bite.  The other thing is the Gray is a very dusty bird that has this very fine dander they use to keep clean with fewer baths.  While not needing so many baths is good it also means it tends to be quite dusty around the birds cage or perch.  Sadly the Amazon while very friendly with me and other men for some reason it did not like women at all and would actively attack them (including my wife) and when we had kids I was just not willing to risk the bird injuring an infant so it had to go.  I truly miss having the parrot now but always think fondly of the time I had with them in my life.

Haven't thought about it in years, but this reminds me of when I was a kid. 
There was an elderly lady, named Gladys, that lived across the street. She had an African Grey. She told me the story of how years ago before she moved in, she saved Sunny as a baby bird. She saw this bush being attacked by these large crows, shooed them away, and found Sunny. 
That bird was a firecracker, and half the time you didn't know whether to be shocked or offended at her extensive vocab lol. 
In the end, them finding each other just made sense. Both tough as nails survivors, but absolutely devoted to those that treated them like family. Both lived super long lives. 
Now as an adult looking back, I'm grateful to have known them both. 🙏🧓🦜🥲🥰

text_content: I'm temporarily staying in Turkey and I saw an African Grey parrot in a cage outside a jewellery store today. Turkey has quire a warm climate, but it's still winter here and people are wearing winter coats... I bought a banana and fed it to the parrot. After eating some of it, he forgot about the rest and all he wanted was affection from me and my husband. We stayed with him for around 15 minutes, scratched him and told him how beautiful he was. He became visibly happier, ruffled his feathers, his pupils started changing size and he even started to dance. I told his owner (who also owns the jewellery store) that he cannot be kept outside during winter because he may get a respiratory disease. She didn't speak any English, but I used a translator. She seemed surprised and grateful for the advice and she brought the cage inside the store. Still, it's so frustrating. You shouldn't get these beautiful and intelligent animals if you don't know how to care for them. They are a huge responsibility - they are like children. The same goes for any other kind of animal. Please, please, don't get one before you educate yourself on how to care for them! They are sentient beings and very much capable of suffering both physically and mentally. They are not toys or just something pretty to look at.

text_content:
Decades ago I had a rescued gray parrot named Zoe. She was amazing. I would take her in the shower to sit on the curtain rod because the humidity helped open her pin feathers. I was washing my hair, and she asked me if I wanted my head scratched (I was scrubbing my scalp). 

She would trick our dog, and insult my brother (but no one else, as he was the only person who insulted her). She loved rock music, and would request specific snacks by name. She definitely understood what people were saying, and she understood what she was saying too, not just mimicking sounds. If I ever owned another bird, it would be a gray. They're so smart.

text_content: I just got my 2 parrots as rescues from the parrot trust Scotland called Cody and coco. In 4 weeks Cody has managed to stop taking haldol medication for the first time in years and coco has opened up to men when before she would only interact with women. I’m so proud of the work I’ve done. They where both caged parrots and didn’t want to come out their cages for anything. Not food motivated so was tough. Now when I go in to their bedroom they literally bang their beaks on the cage to get out and come be where I am. It’s been one of the best experiences of my life and it’s very hard but the reward out weighs any amount of work. They would bite me and cause bleeding and bruising at the start. They really did not want anything to do with anything that wasn’t being alone in their cage. So for anyone worrying about this please don’t. If you are willing to PROVE To them you will care for them no matter what and no matter how much they bite and scratch you you’ll still love them and be kind to them I used to sit and eat dinner in front of their cage or I’d sit watching videos on the laptop on the floor and talk to them even tho they acted like they hated me. Now I’m their absolute everything. Please If u can help them and are willing to work they can open up and love again

text_content:
My aunt and uncle have a beautiful cockatoo! I desperately wanted one when I was younger, so I spent as much time with Bonita (yeah they don’t get points for name creativity😂❤) as I could!
Until I was put in charge of her for 2weeks when I was a teenager, while my aunt and uncle went on a cruise for their anniversary….
Bonita was chill for about the first 4-5 days (which was previously the longest time her “parents” had ever been gone and left me in charge of her before this cruise)— after that, all heII broke loose.  
She totally stopped eating(yes, she went on a hunger strike), she “screamed” almost nonstop and yelled for “mom/dad” (which was heartbreaking), she started destroying everything around the house that she could—and finally, she started ripping feathers out. 
After about 3 days of pure Bonita anarchy, they had to end their trip early and pay for a flight back home before the cruise was even over—because I simply couldn’t get Bonita to calm down, no matter what I tried. 

Sure as the sun rises, almost immediately after they get home—she starts eating normally, no longer shrieked all day and stopped destroying everything in sight!

I still love cockatoos (and Bonita) but I definitely reevaluated my desire for one after that. 😂❤
That was about 25 years ago and they still have never attempted to take another trip that long again, because they don’t want to risk another Bonita meltdown.

text_content:
As an Aussie, we see a lot of escaped/released pet cockatoos - you can tell  because they're usually solo not in a flock and they're extra social and less fearful of people. We have one that occasionally visits us - it's very pointed about telling us what it likes in our garden! Any time we plant parsley, it's mysteriously ripped out and usually deposited onto our downstairs neighbour's balcony! More than once, we've had to knock on their door and ask if we could our plant back please! 

In a flock, they're one of the funniest birds ever - imagine a small child trying to do acrobatics...whilst eating...and talking... They're upside down, food in one claw, usually screeching up a storm and often trying to shoo another cockatoo away with their wings. Then times that by about 100 and put it in the most publicly visible place. It is a magnificent and hilarious sight to behold!

text_content:
Thank you for this video, especially your emphasis on how much responsibility and dedication these birds require to keep properly.

When I was a kid I was friends with someone whose parents owned a couple of cockatoos. The birds were very neglected and it made me incredibly sad. I never saw them leave their cages, and those cages were in a walk-in-closet sized room covered in bird dust, so they were extremely poorly socialized. The larger of the cockatoos would let me stroke him through his cage if I was careful, the smaller would hiss and attempt to bite if I came anywhere near her enclosure. Apparently the owners initially took better care of them, my friend told me when she was young the birds were frequently let out of their enclosures and would even accompany the owners on car rides around town (not the safest thing but at least the birds were getting attention and activity). I suspect the owners got tired of attending to the birds' needs after the novelty wore off or after the owners had children.

Animals are a commitment, not a decoration or a toy to be tossed aside when they get boring or when life gets stressful; people need to know what they're getting into and make sure they're capable of providing for an animal for its entire life, *especially* for animals that are high-needs. I am glad to see some other commenters here have changed their minds about getting a pet cockatoo, and I hope anyone who is still interested in owning one will understand how much time and work they'll need to put in to take proper care of these amazing birds.

NAILED IT!  Yours is one of the best no sugar-coating videos I've seen. Great job! 
Just got back from getting one of my rescued pteradactyls his regular talon trims and dropping another bill on treats, toys, etc. That doesn't scratch the surface in just this month alone. That unyielding cockatoo affection can easily shift into breeding behavior too. That's always fun 😬. I adore my little flock of rescued pteradactyls, however, I don't advocate for psitticines - including parakeets -being part of the pet trade. The number of hookbills relinquished once they reach maturity is heartbreaking. How dare they behave like parrots?! It is literally impossible to meet their very unique needs. I do my best to make sure that everyday is better than the life they had before coming here. I have an aviary for nice days, but it certainly doesn't encompass acres of jungle. I'm in Ohio lol. Thank you again for stating true facts regarding avian husbandry - especially C2's lol. They're all amazing, brilliant creatures. Well I'm off to go figure out if that's chop in my hair or poop. It's probably poop. Again.

text_content: I worked at a doggy daycare and my boss had a couple cockatoos.  She was the perfect owner for one tho, well knowledged about all animals, single and lonely as mean as that might sound, and lived in a pet shop so she had the perfect place to store the parrots.    You can’t put into perspective how intimidating they are tho, sure they might act cute and talk but anytime that beak is near you there is a chance you’ll get bit.    I used to have to sweep the floor and the cockatoos would follow me around the whole time If they were out their cage trying to bite my legs.    If they were in the cage anytime I tried to sweep under a cage I had a massive beak sticking between the bars trying to bite me or the broom.     Not to mention the when they are out of the cage it is the loudest screeching you’ll ever hear, just constant parrot squawking at a 10/10 volume level for as long as they want to squawk which can be hours.    I love cockatoos to death but I’d never consider owning one but any chance I ever get to interact with one I’ll take the opportunity.

text_content:
Great info! Pretty much completely accurate. I would add a few things (as someone who's worked at a specialty bird shop for 7+ years)-

1. You mentioned cockatoos are very dusty. If anyone in the household has asthma, the cockatoo will most likely trigger allergies. Even people who aren't allergic can actually develop allergies to their birds. We've been trying to rehome a goffins cockatoo whose parents LOVED her, but her mom developed lung issues and cannot be in close proximity to birds anymore. Three other cockatoos this year have been surrendered just to our shop for similar health issues (and more were surrendered for other reasons) 

2. Cuddling a cockatoo all the time can cause horrible behavioral issues. Be careful how you cuddle a cockatoo, as petting them in certain areas or giving them too many kisses can make them hormonal. Not only is it frustrating and confusing for the bird, they will lash out and bite.

3. Having multiple birds to keep each other company is not as simple as buying multiple birds. They will have to start in separate cages, be supervised when together, and you will have to make sure it is safe before letting them live in the same cage together.

4. Cockatoos can be extremely jealous. If they choose you as their favorite person, they may be aggressive to your loved ones or anyone they perceive to be an obstacle to your affection. Unfortunately, sometimes you will have to choose between your loved ones or your bird.

video_description: Bogie and I get lots of questions regarding what it's like to own a cockatoo. From screeching loudly to attacking faces, owning a ...
text_content:
This is a great vid. It explains everything a too mama goes thru. Yeah I thought I had my bird skills down, but a cockatoo will make you question that once or twice... at least. Galahs maybe more. Lol. 

Granted I have a very calm and cooperative female. But is a huge part of your day, and like ours get music and dance lessons and such. But cockatoos will make you wonder at times, if you have what it takes as a bird mom, lol. So good job explaining that. All good stuff to consider before... research, research, RESEARCH! That’s what I always say. I spent a decade learning before I adopted mine, but also had been learning my whole life really cuz wanted one as a kid. But I wanted to do it right. And even then you’re still not prepared when it comes to toos... cuz you’ve just adopted a human toddler basically. 

I remember for 10 years I heard a lot in the bird community when I’d inquire about a too (I wanted to take my time and wait for the right one, & that totally worked, I knew her when I saw her. And that’s clearly obvious with you 2 as well). 

But experts would often say “most people aren’t cockatoo people. Only a certain few bird moms.” So I kept learning, but also kept thinking I was probably one of them and it would happen one day. 

Cuz it takes a special type of bird mom. Turns out I was one of those peeps where cockatoo and I just go well together... and now it’s my life (but I love it!), & I’m even doing a study on cockatoo behavior in captivity,.. in a normal home life with cockatoos. To develop a better guide for their emotional and psychological needs. 

I’ve cone to the conclusion they need a totally integrated family life as if they were a toddler. All of the learning, all of the enrichment, etc. Youre clearly one of us too, lol. Cuz now you all of these things too and it’s great you take him to bible study and such. it’s beautiful tbh. Passing down all you’ve learned. Well done on this vid. 

Did we remember to get as one last reason: 

Cockatoos have a way of seeing into your soul, and psyching you out that leaves you feeling rattled and unnerved, like they just powered into your soul and made notes for later... it’s creepy when it happens. Cuz that one is a good one, and how you handle that moment when it occurs might determine the rest of your relationship with that bird. cuz they’re that smart! 

They do this look that conveys both that they just saw into your soul AND that they then might use what they saw against you later, at the same time, and it can be unnerving when you first experience it... I’m sure you’ve dealt with that by now. That look where they just mentally documented something they saw in you that you didn’t see yourself... and are gonna save that for later. Lol. 😂 

I remember going to friends when that first happened, crying “omg I didn’t think she could get to me like that! Like I let her get into my head... Holy crap!” 😂😂😂😂😂🙈 “and now Idk what she might be planning!” Thank goodness other cockatoo moms quickly told me, “you’re ok, you got this, you finally got the Soul Stare & now you’re feeling really inadequate as a bird mom. We’ve all been there.” Like they knew cuz they’d been thru it too. Lol. So having a cockatoo friends support system when you first adopt is also a good idea. So I’m adding that. You’ll have questions. 

Now I know what do in those situations, and it rarely occurs cuz she’s given up trying. I just do it right back & then she gets unnerved, lol... she did it twice in the 1st year... had me questioning all my skills as a bird mom. 😂😂😂🙈 

My girl also loves Dance Moms & I give her dance lessons... she has goals in life. I’m like “at least she has that psych-out stare down, cuz every dance kid learns that for competition and this little too is a master at it!” And then she realized when JoJo Siwa did the exact same psych-out stare on DWTS this week and cheered. 😂🤷🏼‍♀️ All of Abby’s kids learn that for competition. I just hate when she does it to me. Lol. 

So we do a lot of toddler type enrichment. Right now the birds are happily raising their own interactive cyber pet (toy) & learning ABC’s & the arts. We get kids toys for them along with their birdie chew toys. They’re learning to play a tiny piano and everything. The more we do like that the more I watch their personalities grow and things go smoothly with momsplaing why we have certain rules and stuff.

text_content:
Ah! The painted toe nails! I paint usually only nails on my hands and for a while I was puzzled by switches in my cockatiels from "love you mom!" to "DON'T YOU EVER COME NEAR ME", when I finally figured each time it was when I painted my nails a different color! Also the eye peck :D having cockatiels, which are smaller, I still perceive it as cute. 
Also thank you for the video overall. I clicked on it just because I wanted to see how it is owning a galah, and so many points are similar to my cockatiels (except for size)... And the experiences are so similar! One day I think I am managing well, the next day my male cockatiel gets hormonal cause change in light day or something and boom! Protect! The! Pillow! Fort! Attack! Parrot! Activated! I have also two females, and with them so far I've never experienced this nesting behavior though, so I wonder if female galahs are also somewhat tamer with regards to the nesting behavior.

text_content: Sharing your life with a Cockatoo is a lifestyle. Their demands will take over your life. I had 10 years experience working with parrots, including working in an aviary before deciding to adopt a Mollucan. We did our research including visiting bird sanctuaries and even the National zoo. I still had no idea what we were in for when we brought him home. As the video states they have the intelligence of a human toddler and share many of the same traits. They throw tantrums, they throw food, they bite, and their scream has been measured at 110db  (compared to 130db for a jet engine at 100 feet). He's been our faithful companion for over 38 years, but I know I speak for both of us when I say I there are days we wish we didn't have him.

I was looking for a celebes.

Male allso owns me now and if I want to spend some time with the female, we must go to another room 😅

text_content: Cockatoos are famously known for bonding with one and only 1 family member. They will actively attempt to keep you separated from the member they've bonded with. They will mock charge, they will bite and will even fake an injury to drive the bonded person directly to them. They are not a family bird. If you're looking for a family birds i recommend the Eclectus. They are calm, they want to bond with the entire family and they want to be included in family activities. They are truly great family birds.

video_description: The Triton Cockatoo is one of the largest Sulphur Crested Cockatoos and are extremely popular for people wanting a very large ...
text_content:
I have had birds for 25 years. I had one cockatiel for 20 years, then another for 3 (died of a random seizure) 3 budgies (years so far) and an Indian ring neck (1 year so far but she is 4 years old).

Birds absolutely know how you feel about certain things and they do troll you and test your patience purely for their own entertainment. They can outsmart you. I never have had an aggressive bird. All of mine have been friendly but a bored bird is a destructive bird and a loud bird. If you can't handle a cockateil you have no chance at a cockatoo. Indian ringnecks are 5x more of a handful then a cockatiel and if you can't handle a ring neck you have no chance at a cockatoo. Birds a fraction of the intelligence of a cockatoo can still know exactly how to troll you. You think training will be easy, the moment you teach them you don't want something they now know exactly how to mess with you. They might mess with you just to get more treats. Its easy for a bird to take whatever lesson you teach them however they want. It's like a freind who just reads meaning and misinterpretation in your words for their own entertainment. This isn't them being malicious they just need the stimulation. It is nolever a good idea to try to punish the behavior. They are just being Birds. It is like having toddlers. You will never have complete control and they are not obedient animals as a default. You can train them but it's nothing like most dogs. If there is an analogous dog, it might be a border collie. They have their own instinct and choose when they listen. If you dispositionally need control never get a bird. If you need order never get a bird.

video_title: 15 Tips For Cockatoo Owners!
video_description: Rocket the lesser sulphur crested cockatoo attends one of our masterclasses and we share 15 tips for cockatoo owners, but they ...
text_content:
Awesome cockatoo and such a sensitive thoughtful owner!! It’s very possible she has actually trained the lack of enthusiasm her bird was showing with the cup trick. If you repeat unenthusiastic training even a few times with negative energy (trainer frustration and bird frustration) - well, practice makes perfect with both performance (quitting) and emotional state (discouraged, frustrated, averse to training). 

I would put away the cup trick for now and only train extremely short sessions with very easy kindergarten behaviors and great enthusiasm. Put him back in his cage when he’s done just 3 great reps of one trick. After a break, out again for a quick HOT training! Build back his great excitement for training. 

When you start back with any challenging behavior like the cup trick do your homework FIRST! On paper, break it down into tiny steps. If you break it down into steps he can easily achieve with almost no errors it’s a huge boost for his emotional value for training. 

Bird errors ALWAYS mean, “Oh shoot, I screwed up. I need to rethink my training steps. Let me immediately ask for a behavior he knows well, jackpot, and have a Yay You Did Great! party. This bird showed so many indications of how confused it was. Confusion kills training joy. But it can be reversed! By a wonderful trainer like this owner.

text_content: I've been around moluccan  cockatoos for upwards of 30 years. The one thing I noticed is you cannot be frightened of them, they will take advantage of that. At present I have six of them. Two females, Dolly and Pumpkin, that would never even consider biting me. One other female, April, gives me a pinch occasionally but it's only if I'm not paying enough attention. I have one small male, Marin, that used to be a bit aggressive but now that he's approaching 30 years old he's settled down. Two other 15-year-old males, Louie and huckleberry, we're passed around to different homes over the years because they were a bit aggressive but now that they've become part of our family they are just the most wonderful sweethearts.  Cockatoos are awesome. Oh, and it will be a much happier experience if you're handy at finish carpentry.

text_content: I have been held captive by an umbrella cockatoo for at least 12 years now. I am usually happily held captive. Sometimes he is a bit loud-usually waiting for a crucial time during a tv show. He is so friendly and in no way has been influenced by my own anti-social behavior and my preference for no other humans around. He talks to my cat and other pets and greets them when they meet. Any stranger or serial killer who knocks at the door is greeted by a loud friendly 'Hello' His 'beak of iron strength' has destroyed metal dishes and cage bars, so far, he has never had to reprimand me with his beak as I show him proper respect at all times. He has trained me well.  Cockatoos are not for everyone, but so far, my captor and I seem to be happy and healthy. Did you know that most umbrellas are left footed? btw I love him dearly. (Could be Stockholm syndrome.)

text_content: We have one we adopted , 22 year old male.   And we discovered he has a toll, you can pay in time spent around him,  or in blood.  The more blood you spill by holding him,  the faster he will come around to be your friend.   I'm his favorite and basically as since day 1 which was VERY odd,  he attached right to me I must look something like his former owner.  But randomly he would still just bite me as if he realized I was not who he thought.   But within a year we had a good relationship.   My wife and or any woman he seems to really REALLY hate, and has even jumped off his cage to attack my mother and mother in law.   As for my wife, she reduced the time cost by adding a little blood to it and they are also friends now. And it helps a lot to read the bird,   I made games out of everything he did, when he would get upset and start making high pitched scream sounds, I would lightly tap him on his back and make a " bock bock bock " sound,  and at some point that became a game, and he quit doing it.   Also Bobble Bobble, Kirby Coaster (flip him upside down and back) and others.   His screams we were able to replace with him barking.    We learned he hated the spray bottle we would use to mist him,  so we used it as threat not a bath and he would quit screming, but he replaced screaming himself with barking like our dogs.   So now he barks at things he don't like, and we are okay with that since its MUCH lower volume lol.

text_content: Here on my farm here in Australia. I've had the same wild sulphur crested cockatoo visiting me for over 35 years. It was a young bird when I was young and I used to put out sunflower seeds and water for it and other birds. I would sit on the bottom step of my homestead and hold my hand out with seeds for them. After many tries the young cockatoo came and ate out of my hand. And from then on, it got more and more comfortable with me and eventually it would sit on my arm and eat the sunflower seeds. It would stay around the homestead yard most of the day  either sleeping in a tree, swimming in the large birdbath or flying around. I eventually taught it to talk and say hello. For a wild animal its more like a pet. I don't feed it every day but he (in sure it's a male) comes to visit and is very placed and gentle and doesn't bite.  He plays around with my dogs and often comes with his entire family.  I'm very lucky to have a wild animal stay around for so long. 😊

text_content:
Can I just say... this parent knows what she's doing. Like any pet and kid, it's a hit/miss situation whether the kid and the pet get along or not, birds especially. I absolutely love how this mom has helped strengthen the bond between the cockatoo and the baby. I think the thing I love most about this was that the mom got toys of the bird to help the kid to not be scared of him. Birds with those kinds of beaks and of that kind of size can scare some people, so I'm really happy that that mom was able to make the bird seem like a friend in that way. And I'm really happy that she's supervising them when together, that way they won't hurt the other. She knows what she's doing. 

I also loved that she knew to dote on both the baby and bird equally. It's SO imperative that this happens. They're both kids in a way and both deserve that love and affection. 

I truly hope that the kid and the cockatoo will stay buddies for many years to come. Great job, Mom!

text_content:
As a parrot owner of 27 years - THANK YOU for giving the negative points to owning a parrot.  Everyone shows the cutesy, cuddly parts of life with a parrot, but rarely do they give the warnings.  I studied them for a year before I decided on a Congo African Grey.  I knew I would get bitten.  I knew they required time and attention.  I have many scars on my fingers, and one or two on my lips.  We love our boys, but we also understand them.  You HAVE to be able to read body language.  Greys are not as attention seeking a 'toos are, but they can be very aggressive during mating season - spring & fall.  They do not allow deviation from a set schedule, and they need a schedule.
Our orange-winged Amazon is very sweet and gentle.  The only time he bites is when there is a child in the house.  When a child enters, he no longer wants much to do with me.  He wants to cuddle with the child.  Otherwise he is patient, he is sweet - but when he screams - he makes your eardrums shake.  And he screams fairly often.
I really appreciate you putting it out there so people know what they are getting into.

text_content: My brother had a Red Lored Amazon.  He loved men and hated women (nobody told my brother this).  He would be sweet and funny in a room full of men and see a woman out the window and his eyes would dilate in and out and his voice would sound satanic and then he would flip out and attack the closest person to him.  He trapped my male friend in a bedroom because he saw a women knock on the front door and we had to "rescue" my friend.  We found a home for him with a gay couple who knew what he was like and they still have him to this day - 40 years later.

text_content: I have one and i regret getting one everyday, my family decided to get African gray so we went to a pet shop and bought a new born African gray, the bird turned out to be disabled and it had heart problems so the shop gave us our money back. They offered us a baby Umbrella cockatoo instead and we took it. The cockatoo was a female and she was so big compared to regular cockatoos. We didn’t know how bad cockatoos were before we bought them we just had conures before. My pet cockatoo is 7 now and she just has so many issues. She gets super sexual with us so we cant touch her below the neck, we learned that the hard way after she laid 4 eggs between the ages of 4-6💀 terrible ik. We trained her to stay on her cage with our other bird which is a nanday conure. They get alone just fine and rarely fight but when they do she always does something horrible like once she bite a hole in the other birds beak. She has never not once bit a human before though. She so loving and sweet but also a demon at the same time. She has gotten out and ruined tv remotes and cords before. She also has butt problems because she prolapse herself whenever she feels like she doesn’t have enough attention. We had to result into spraying her with water because she pushes her organ out if she doesnt get sprayed. She has been doing her butt thing for years now and she never cleans her butt because she started at a young age. Not her fault at all by the way she has behavior issues because we gave her too much attention. I was like 10 when we got her and I’m 17 now. Lover her to death but she would be better off in the wild 😂. She’s not aggressive at all and she is well trained, if we say go up she runs up her cage, if we say get in the cage she runs right in.  We fixed her sexual issue by not letting her on the couch with us. She has to stay on the cage, once in a whole we’ll let her sit on our knee on the couch but other then that she stays on her cage. She chose my father from day one because he never feared her at all and he is also the person she fears the most. She loves my dad but also fears my dad so much, we don’t abuse her at all btw she just fears my dad because he has a big beard and a deep voice and he’s very tall. She has eacaped the cage so many times but recently she has been escaping her cage and staying on top of her cage with the other bird which is progress 😂. She’s just starting to become better because we try to not cuddle her or anything and she talks sooooo much. She got her butt problem original from a stomach illness btw, it started from that and we gave her attention when she would push her stuff out, now she thinks that’s like the bell that get’s everyone’s attention. She is a handful and i regret wanting her lol. (Edit) btw i just wanted to mention she has a huge cage with a nice special structure on top where she chills for most of the day and it’s in the living room so she sees us all day and she gets attention all the time.

text_content: As a carer for a cockatoo I found your video so true,  one thing i will mention that I have found especially with my Greater crested sulphur cockatoo called Buster but also with other species of cockatoos that I have cared for, is how he will pretend to like a visitor,  No matter how often I warn my visitors not to trust his sweet ways, He will act so sweet and charming just to lure them closer so he can bite them, as you know his bite can be nasty.  I keep him caged when I have Visitors but he will act so nice and loving that he has bitten five visitors.  He is so intelligent  you can see his thought process but Too's can move there head so fast from a head bowed down to bite the finger is so fast and quicker than a humans reaction.  I have never been bit by a Cockatoo and I can do anything with Buster but he is a one person bird.  I have over 50 birds in my home and most flying free,  His cage was for a green winged macaw but the strength in his beak has bent some of the bars and removed one,  How ever He gets on with the other flock members providing they are not cockatoos or eclectus parrots and most nights two of my budgies and a pair of zebra finches will get through the bars to sleep next to him, also three of my ringnecks and two of my amazons will sleep on top of his cage at night.   Thank you for making this video and for the love and understanding of your birds,

text_content: I've had a cockatoo and I've had a blue front Amazon.  I would never let them be close to each other. Cockatoos are extremely intelligent birds. They have the emotional intelligence of a 5-year-old. Their  jealousy drives them to harm or kill their rival which could be a guinea pig or a hamster, or a cat or a dog.  They will go after them if they think that you're not giving them enough attention and love. I ended up giving our cockatoo to a vet lab technician who had always wanted a cockatoo and she had no other animals so it's a perfect home for him.  So this cockatoo is not showing dominance only, he is showing that he will attack and he will harm that bird.  The cockatoo I had, named Kazoo,  had to be pulled off of my African Grey, named Pepper, and he had punctured her cheek and her wing.  And that was not the first incident I had with a cockatoo he went after my darling little Harley bird who is what we call a pocket parrot.  He hurt Harley so badly in his wing we had to amputate the wing as it was destroyed. Harley was a feisty little parrot and he climbed off of his cage one day and marched over to Kazoos cage because he was going to finish what Kazoo had started and I'm so glad I was there to scoop him up and lock him back in his cage or he would have been killed that day.  Something I find very appalling on the internet with animals is people get animals they don't understand they don't know anything about their breed or what the needs are for that individual animal.  I handled Birds since I was 10 years old and I'm in my 70s now and I am appalled at how little people understand are know about birds they are highly intelligent more so than a dog is believe it or not.

video_description: Victoria was given six months to live. Rescued from a life of neglect, this cockatoo was weak, dehydrated, and silenced by years in ...
text_content:
It's amazing how smart birds can be. I've had several beautiful birds that bonded with me. Of my flock of four laying hens one even recognized I was hurt when I strained my back repairing a fence after a tornado. As I was laying on my back until the pain subsided she came to me, stroked my side with her foot, climbed on my chest, and nestled in my armpit to comfort me. 

A beautiful Great Snowy Egret waits in sight of the park gate where me, and my dog enter on his daily long walk. On my bicycle with the dog running beside me she'll fly, sometimes right next to my shoulder, with us. 

A Blue Heron attracted to eat the fish drawn to the bread when I feed the Canadian Geese, and Mallard Ducks bonded with me. Feeding right by my knee for years she twice hopped to me, and gave me a fish two days in a row.

text_content:
This story should also serve as an incentive to demand that _all_ "animal breeders" be recognized as the evil @$$holes they truly are.

Animal "breeding" should be *_harshly_* regulated, if even permitted at all, regardless of which species is  held captive. 

They should all be subjected to frequent unnanounced checks by veterinarians and law enforcement to ensure they can't do to even one more bird or other animal what was done to Victoria the Cockatoo.

If any "breeder" is found to be abusing, neglecting, and forcing any animal to subsist in suboptimal conditions, the animals should be immediately seized and evacuated to safety, and the captor charged with every single violation and ruthlessly prosecuted. 

And one such epidode should permanently preclude them from EVER having any more animals in their clutches ever again, either. 

*I do not care one iota* about the "Oh, poor me, it's my livelihood!" whining as an excuse to abuse, torture, deprive, and neglect other species held as *_slaves,_* forced to live in misery each and every day because some lazy, greedy, and reckless @$$hole is using animal misery to make money.

Screw that, and screw them. 

Those types deserve to suffer the way they make other beings suffer, and I don't care who's feelings get hurt reading this, either. 

If you make your money on the backs of captive slaves, then _you deserve to suffer equal consequences for your crimes._

Victoria's life was significantly shortened due the abuse and torture she suffered. 

_There is no excuse that will ever suffice,_ no second chances deserved, no mercy extended, and no f*cks given about @$$holes who treat animals the way Victoria was treated. 

I just can't care about anyone who whines about how they they are "overegulated" by authorities when they are greedy, abusive pieces of 💩!

text_content:
As a owner of a parrot for 12 years:
1.) Macaws are the LOUDEST parrots in the rainforest. So they will scream EXTREMELY LOUD when they play, want attention, are upset, when they want your food, ect.
2.) Their intelligence is the equivalent to a human toddler. They need as much attention, love, & play as a human toddler. If you don't give them this time they will have significant mental health & physical healthy problems.
3.) They go through puberty just like humans. Once the reach puberty (in their early teens) their personality changes. They will become more aggressive, jealous if u hangout with anyone else, and randomly biting. 
4.) They poop on EVERYTHING constantly. They poop every 15 minutes so you have to be ok with them pooping on ur furniture, nice house items, & with deep cleaning the cage every couple of days.
5.) Vet bills are EXTREMELY EXPENSIVE. The average vet bill for me was $400. When she became sick it was around $1,000 each vet visit for mediations & tests.
6.) Their average lifespan in captivity is the human life span. If something happens to you or if u adopt a young macaw they will out live you. They have SEVERE PTSD if they have to switch owners because of the deep emotional connection. This can lead to self mutilation.
7.) They love only 1 human at a time. They will be jealous & hate any other humans you are close to.

text_content: A while ago my mom impulse bought a blue and gold from someone. When we first got it I was probably around 9-10. He’s almost the same age as me. I’m 14 now and until recently I wanted nothing to do with him. But my parents started talking about rehoming him and that’s when I realized I actually like him a lot. My parents have done nothing to train him or even spent much time with him so he screams and bites. My parents always start yelling at him when he screams and to calm him down all I have to do is go up and talk to him. This video has inspired me to help him out and get him to become friendly. I’m going to start working with him on a daily basis. I’ve noticed that he’s always been very interested and curious about me. I feel like I can make his life a lot better.
