text_content:
Im an animal behaviourist who specialises in macaws, and I have to say that for 18 months, an unresearched impulse buy, you are doing great! As to all the things he does that you dont love very much (carpet removal, door pruning, screeching, grabbing, etc) these are things that are easily fixed with help from a parrot behaviourist, in person or via live video on pm, etc. Lessons that you, your partner and Mikey all get to learn together, problem solving where you all have a piece of the puzzle.
I would recommend you finding a macaw behaviorist sooner rather than later, to help you fix the negative issues. MY blue and gold wears a harness and goes everywhere I do, as he is a heart monitor and pre warns before I'm going to collapse, so believe me when I say your Mikey is SMART, and being a little rotter pushing the boundries with you, playing at "dumb birdy" ...and conned you successfully! Welcome to parronthood 😁 (some days your fid is just going to be a d@#&) best thing you can do atm is learn his real capabilities re understanding you both, and what his iq and eq ratings are, then tailor his learning appropriately.
I am worried about your mesh by the way. It doesnt look stainless steel and anything else is poisonous to macaws in particular... It is why most bird rooms have only free standing perches and those hung from the ceiling (hook screwed through a frisbee so it cant be chewed out of ceiling) and all lights and windows covered with perspex or stainless cages, and a security screen door in place of the bedroom door!
Same with most bird toys you buy... licking and chewing at chrome chains kill macaws.

text_content: I'm gonna be honest, I agree with the no clipping. My mother has raised me to believe that owning birds that can fly is cruel, unless you have a space where they can fly. They were made to fly so why prevent them from doing so? If you don't want your pet to fly then why the hell would you get a flying pet? And I agree with the window thing. People say they clip their bird's wings so they won't fly out the window. Why the hell is their window open? It's all so stupid to me to be honest.I do admit that I have had to clip wings. But it was the wings of a small chicken. I never expected her to fly like she did and we have neighbors with dogs. It broke my heart to have to clip her wings but chickens weren't exactly made for flying in the first place. (And we didn't know how flighty her breed was when we got her.)In the end, if you don't have a place for your bird to fly and it's not safe, don't get a flying bird. Just don't do it! Let someone who can take care of a flying bird do it! It's that simple. If you love them, you'll let them fly. If it's not safe where you are, be caring enough to let them fly elsewhere.

text_content: My parents bought a macaw before they had children. Macaws & children DON'T mix. My parents had the macaw locked up in a cage for 17 years because macaws can't handle children so she would bite the us. Macaws can bite through broomsticks so you can imagine how bad the bites were. When I turned 12 I "saved her" & developed a relationship with her. We were insperatable until she passed away 2 years ago. So I took care of her for 12 years. So PLEASE do NOT get a macaw if you plan on having children. While in the cage she plucked out the majority of her feathers out of stress of being locked up 24/7. The feathers never grew back & she developed PTSD. Please don't get a macaw if you want children

text_content: Wonderful video on how to be a great friend to these awesome birds. I am a board member for Exotic Bird Rescue of Oregon (USA) and am blessed with a loving Catalina Macaw myself. People, please listen very closely to this message. It is wonderfully put. On average, exotic birds are re-homed, more than 5 times before finally finding a 'forever' home. and the effects on them can be horrendous. Everything from self-mutilation (feather plucking and even tearing holes in their own flesh) to nearly un-controllable screaming and aggression. (Nearly...  a lot of work and love can remedy a lot of problems).  Also, find a bird club in your area and get some experience with them. Learning to read body language can save the bird and YOU a lot of blood and tears.  I only have one... small... disagreement, and that is with clipping wings and free flighting. I agree that clipping wings seem a travesty but there are situations where, for the safety of the bird, I would recommend it. Also being part of a rescue I can assure you that even well-trained birds get scared and fly away on occasion... sometimes never to be seen (alive at least) again. We get several calls a week regarding birds that 'have never flown off before' having been scared and are gone....it is heartbreaking. If you do take your bird outside....please PLEASE think about an appropriate flight harness.

text_content:
I grew up in a macaw household. They can live to 100 in captivity, and often outlive their owners. My parents had 3 when I was born, and they re-homed 2 of them because 4 children was too much for them. As a result, I had a scarlet macaw named Kimba in my home until she passed from a stroke when I was about 4. I still remember my mom in tears, holding the colorfulbird in her arms, unable to save her. Not long after, we purchased a B&G macaw from a breeder. I remember walking into the "pet store" in Hollywood, and seeing several pin-feathered birds on a table. That was 1983. The pin-feathered creature we took home that day became my bird brother, and still lives with my dad. He has arteriosclerosis, and must be medicated to prevent/delay a fatal stroke.

They are incredible animals, with the mentality of a human 2y/o for their entire lives. They can be very loving and cuddly, protective and loyal, and super destructive, all in the same day. If you're thinking of adopting a macaw, treat the decision with the same seriousness as the thought of adopting a special-needs human child. It takes that much dedication, and someone else will need to be ready to take on that responsibility when you die.

text_content:
My RB Cockatoo has arthritis and loves her feet massaged. I'll put coconut oil on her feet everyday, and she's learned to "ask" for the oil by lifting up one of her feet when I'm giving her loves (aka scritches on her head).

Speaking of a bird's head, when they are molting, the shafts get pretty sharp. The bird's head is more sensitive than normal during this time. It's a good idea to gently break the shafts apart by rubbing each of them between the thumb and forefinger. Otherwise, the feathers poke them while you're petting them. This is painful and that can result in a bite, too.

text_content:
We just rescued a blue and gold Macaw named Roscoe. We were told he we *roughly* 14 years old, but the people we got him from said they only had him for four years, so they're aren't even sure. 

He had quite an attachment to me very quick, which is crazy because I've never been a bird owner before until I met my Fiancée, who hand raised and sold birds as a teenager and has dealt with them her whole life. 

But he is very territorial and will bark and scream at us if we get too close or hug and kiss on one another. And if I'm not in the room with her, he will snap at her and try to bite, but never does that to me. She hasn't done anything to him, and I haven't done anything special to deserve his undying love and affection. So i was curious if that seems to be typical of them. 

For some experience context for myself; She had a cockatiel when we got together, named Blaiz, and we rescued a blind and arthritic orange winged amazon parrot shortly after we got together, named Peter. Both alive and as well as can be. 
Blaiz is now 15, cranky, old boy, but he belonged to her son, who unfortunately passed away a few months back, so there is 0 chance we get rid of him. 

Peter is 43. He was in a home that didn't care about him. His original owner passed away a few years back from old age, and the lady we got him from didn't want him, nor did she take care of him. His beak was so overgrown that it's now permanently shifted his jaw so his mouth is crooked. And his talons were so overgrown that he couldn't walk on his feet, to which one grew very stiff, and now he can't open it all the way. (Vet said there was nothing we could do about it because it had progressed too far for medicine to be of any use.)

Roscoe is very healthy, aside from the awful diet the last couple had him on, which consisted of peanuts, lots of fried potatoes, and paper. 🙃

We have fixed the diet. He now has a regular diet of fresh fruits and veggies as he should, with a nutriberri blend that we mix plumage food into. And the occasional peanut or pistachio for a treat. 

He's a tricky little guy, but he is a huge sweetheart. He has an attitude, but he is much larger than Peter, so while he has never attacked me, (he snapped at me once, but didn't hurt or even leave a mark, so I'm sure he was just playing) I'm still pretty nervous around him. He let's me pick him up, and pet his head. I haven't tried his face around his eyes yet, so I'm going to try that today and see if he's okay with it. 

I was just wondering if you had any tips for helping him adjust to her? She's always been a huge bird lover, and she was so excited to get Roscoe. He was not very nice to her the first week we had him, and she got very frustrated by him, and actually yelled (her yells aren't very yelly) at him. Blaiz is actually a total asshole because her son didn't really take care of him, and I've seen Blaiz bite her and draw blood and she's never gotten mad enough to yell at him. So I can tell that she's really upset by his behavior, more so because he is so nice to me. I just want to do my best to help him adjust to her, too. 

I'm sorry that was a lot of stuff to say. 😅

text_content:
Hello from August 2020. 
For some crazy reason, YouTube has recently recommended this video to all of you so before you leave your comments, let us answer a few questions. 

Yes, he can fly, very very well, he actually free flies which means he is outside in parks for around an hour every single day soaring through the skies freely with his lover Mia. 

He chooses to walk and not fly up the stairs due to he is smart enough to know how large his wing span is, he knows if he attempted to fly, he won’t have enough space and may hurt himself. 

No, this is not a closet... It was actually a double bedroom, we just choose to keep a clothing rail in it. This aviary has been taken down and a larger powdercoated steel one has been built. 

No, he is not in a dark room with no natural light, there is a large double window in here which in this video is covered up due to it was night time. 

Feel free to watch any of the other videos uploaded over the last 2 years which should show all the points highlighted above. 

Thanks for watching.

text_content: Learning your bird's boundaries is a huge part of things. I think many birds end up in rescue situations because of this. People don't understand that it takes time to learn how to read them and when a bird bites they blame it on the bird being "aggressive" rather than their own lack of patience and understanding. text_content: I work behavioral issue macaws to help them become adoption candidates. This is very true. Most bites I’ve gotten have come from fear. I’ve worked with macaws who have been through insane amounts of abuse, I’ve worked with birds that would bite everyone and everything, yet they never hurt me to the point of needing stitches or permanent disfigurement. I’ve gotten some nasty bites here and there, I’ve collected a few scars even, but it’s a far cry from the real damage these guys can do. They are pretty gentle by nature. I love seeing how they transform and learn better boundaries as well. I learn  how to keep that bird from feeling the need to bite. Birds will bite but most of the time they give very clear warnings before hand. It definitely is about knowing your bird as well, birds can bite not just because they’re scared but also because they are over stimulated. Be gentle, patient, and kind with a biting bird. Most will learn boundaries over time or at least you will learn how to pick up on the warning signs.

text_content:
I will say this as an umbrella cockatoo dad of five years:

My bird (Einstein) is one of the most affectionate and emotionally intelligent, if not needy, animals I’ve ever owned. He explores with his beak and having a safe and fruitful relationship with him has been all about learning how to read his body language, understand his precursors to naughty or stress behavior, and setting/enforcing boundaries when needed. 

However, with that said…. He has bitten me and my sister very badly quite a few times. Being a bird owner is about knowing that yes, you can expect nips, but you need to be prepared for the occasional major bite and approach it with the understanding that going forward…. Learn from WHY you got bit. 

For every single major bite I’ve sustained from Einstein, I can 100% say that it was my fault for not listening to his body language. You have to know your bird inside and out, but to a degree I disagree that you shouldn’t expect proper bites. I find that to be unrealistic 

Love your birds by the way, it’s obvious that you take incredible care of them and know their body languages so so so well. You’re a great bird dad

text_content:
Beautiful baby...her/his flight feathers don't appear to be fully open yet!
I raised B&G, Green Wing, and Military macaws for some years before taking them all down to Panama, releasing them on Isla Coiba before it was established as a National Park in 1991.  I worked with the Panamanian government throughout the '70s--'80s doing research toward establishing the island as a national park...Isla Coiba has the largest density of macaws anywhere in Latin America.  There were times I saw flocks of over 25 macaws flying about this pristine (uninhabited) island. My research there, and other locals was basis for my PhD...until politics intervened my dream was re-establishing macaws in their original range throughout the West Indies.
I've some great tales of rescuing baby Hyacinth macaws from giant trees (illegally) cut down in the Northern Pantanal region...turning them over to indigenous elders who then entrusted them to local children already well accustomed to rearing parrots/macaws...almost got shot several times by parrot poacher, too!  
You hyacinth is clever, beautiful and will give you and your family life-long joy...take care of and protect "her"!

text_content:
I have a very large beautiful male yellow nape  named Cricket.  I have had him for 27 years (he is 28) and I got him from a sanctuary where he'd been placed after being abused.  I think HR is the perfect parrot, but it took a lot of time and work to gain his trust, and he still barely tolerates men.  

He is relatively quiet, and sing and talks and doesn't scream except rarely and only if he senses danger like a hawk or helicopter or cat.and is incredibly loving in his way and extremely affectionate.  I am disabled an have several chronic illnesses including brittle diabetes, and he learned very early to alert me to low blood sugar emergencies by rubbing vigorously on my cheek and saying "it's OK!"  "You alright?" and not stopping until I ate or drank (of course he got jackpot treats!). If it was late or bedtime he would often not go  in his cage, but run to the bedroom and station himself on the headboard of the bed and would want to watch me all night. Getting him in his cage was hard on those nights!   I live in NYC and he is a fixture in the neighborhood,  If he isn't seen for a  few days during good weather,  people come and ask about him.  He constantly flirts with the neighborhood women calling them to. "come see the birdie", and telling them that they're beautiful. His only flaw is that he's messy,but I wouldn't change a thing about him.  He is a true blessing in my life,  and he also brings smiles to almost everyone who sees him!

text_content:
I work in a petshop and we get people abandoning pets more then you would know. A little over a year ago we got an amazon this way, the original owner didnt have the time to take him out of his cage for health reasons. Never had a bird before or wanted one, always saw them as unpredictable. He was biting most people, he was fine with me. Was always the ''get to work early type'' so after a couple weeks founded out he sings to symphonic metal when he was out and listening to music with me. He stole my heart and  i got him home, its crazy how much they give to you, its a commitment, but it is so worth it. We have been together since and its great, he is well known in the neighborhood as we take walks morning and evening when the temperature allows it (Canada). His name is Picchu or ''Sir what are you doing'' when he is causing mayhem. He is around 5 years old (i dont have paper on him)       

My cat is still not super ok with him yet but we are working on it.

video_title: My Yellow Naped Amazon Parrot, George
video_description: Spending Mother's Day with my Feather & Fur Babies This video was not directed to children.
text_content: He reminds me of my Oscar who's 23 now. I've had him 3 and half years. Oscar had a rough life but he's coming into it. Oscar is a bit bigger than your guy. You are lucky as Oscar was abused during his first half of his life. He can be a bully as well mine enjoys his TV programs like an old man. We both have anxiety, we both have PTSD we are both agoraphobic so needless to say we were made to be together lol. We have the same bond. He's my only friend after life went belly up for me. I've always taken in parrots who have had tough backgrounds. I've always helped them get back to a good baseline and find them good homes. But Oscar is definitely here for the long haul. video_title: Everything Painted Buntings Eat (Complete Diet Guide!) | Attracting Them Your Feeder
video_description: Discover everything Painted Buntings eat – from seeds and berries to surprising protein-packed snacks! This complete diet ...
text_content:
Great to hear that you have more buntings this year. For me, this is the best year for painted buntings in the yard. The first wintering one was a female in 2015-16. From the next season though 2019-20 there was one typical male and one female. In 2020-21, a second male, with a very yellow back, with a faint red flecking arrived. The same one (?) has come back each year since. There’s been 2-3 green ones also. Last year, there were 3 female types. One had a bright blue shine around the head. This one was almost always very close to the female cardinal for some reason. The only male I saw was the yellow-backed one, and not regularly like the other three.  I’ll let you know about this year, after some problems, and solutions, I’ve been working out in the last few years.
From 2020-23, I was battling Eurasian Collared-Doves. Never witnessed aggression with them-Instead huge numbers (100 or more at a time!) would show up. Unlike any of the other yard birds, they’d panic for no obvious reason, all the time. This scared everyone else,of course. Not joking, half a bag of seed would be gone in a few hours. In time, I just ended up placing little bits of seed near the window on the ground, and/or stopped entirely for a few days when the doves would show up. This summer, I re-discovered a squirrel-proof bird feeder I bought in 2008. It didn’t attract much of anything back then (squirrels even ignored it). Found it, cleaned it up, and put it up where I could see from my desk. The few collared-doves are still around, as well as mournings, and one white-winged,all ignore the feeder. All the others, including the buntings, use it. Quickly another problem arose. House sparrows also stay away-until seed is put out. Within a week, very large numbers hung around, pretty much all day. Cardinals and blue jays simply pushed them aside. Not so with the buntings. They would drive them off. I would even see a female sparrow just sit on the feeder for long periods, pecking at any bunting that tried to land. Something needed to be done, immediately! I emptied the feeder first. It’s probably not good to have hordes of any species constantly around. So many would attract predators, bird droppings cover everything, and diseases could spread.  Went on YouTube to find a solution. A channel called ‘Badgerland Birds’ had a video that deals with this issue that looked promising. Hanging filaments of silver twine around the feeder deters them. I bent a metal clothes hanger into a circular shape, installing it just above the feeder. Attached several strands to the hanger, tying a heavy screw to the ends of each. This keeps birds from getting tangled in the strands, and also prevents individual pieces from getting tangled up. Not any paid endorsement… but it completely solved the problem! Before I came inside, one of the females was already eating. The sparrows tried to get to the feeder. They’d hover, gather on nearby branches, never actually landing. This was on October 24th. One female alone a few days later managed to get in. After a minute it flew away. Not once since have any others been successful, and now none even try. A few do come, eating from the ground below. Within two days, the cardinals came back to the feeder, and the next day the blue jays. They all come and go normally now. I’m not sure exactly why, but it worked better than I imagined!
As of today (Nov 19th) there are at least eight buntings. For a while, even with the sparrows, one green-backed and one yellow-backed male. There’s been five greens. Sometimes all are around the feeder at the same time. Early on Saturday (16th), there were two green-backed males eating next to each other, with the yellow-backed one a branch a few inches away. Definitely eight. Starting yesterday, a green one with a strong yellow triangle-shaped patch on the back that stands out visits. I don’t think I overlooked it. If not, then there are nine! I work (try to) at my desk a lot, so there’s been ample time to watch the buntings. The two males often come separately, each with two or more greens. They also come from different directions. 
Who knows, there may be a few groups of these birds in my pretty unremarkable Little Havana/ Shenandoah neighborhood. With your numbers, maybe Painted Buntings are doing better. I definitely see larger numbers out birding than back in the 1990s. Finally, some good news?

video_title: Half Moon Conure, BIG Parrot in a TINY body?
video_description: The Half Moon Conure, also known as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
Every conure has their own personalities. 
My female peachy or half moon 🌙 conure only gets noisy if she see's a predator outside. Then she FREAKS OUT ! One other time she gets noisy in a different way, is when she pleasures herself ( which is often) against a inanimate object she will make a weird noise that if you don't see what's she's doing, you'd think she is in Trouble. 
My Peachy does NOT say 1 word. 
I will say Peanut is very inquisitive about things vs my other 13 parrots. She is sooooo smart in that department. 
Her roommate and platonic friend Kiwi, my male Sun Conure is noisy and loud.
The worse thing is his repetitive sound he makes over and over and over. 
There is very little variation in his vocalization.  It drives me absolutely 
crazy ! Like parrot experts tell you,
Do NOT yell at your parrot. It achieves 
NOTHING but a even noisier bird.
Overall, conures are GREAT 1st parrots but I recommend a male over female. 
Males are better tempered. Thats not to say someone can't have a very sweet female. Females WILL eventually lay eggs and once that starts, their little personalities will change from sweet to
aggressive at certain times. I'm not a expert on parrots, but I have enough of them for roughly 15 years to gain some experience/knowledge about them.

text_content:
I’ve had mine for 22 years.  He is a doll but not cuddly, I cannot skritch his head.  He is super smart, a pretty good talker.  Very polite and not that messy for a parrot.  His only loud moments are his alarm call when someone is in the yard or I walk past him with a broom, yardstick, etc.  All conures are “nippy” AKA bitey….lol.  My Peach Fronted only bites under extreme duress.  He has really never bitten me.  When I got him, I asked him “can I call you Swee’Pea” and he said “ok”❤❤   He loves to hang out on my shoulder, he laughs and flirts.  
He loves music and has his own playlist he dances to.  
I think they are awesome little birds.

video_title: Peach Fronted Conure, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Peach Fronted Conure, often confused as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
Oh, Momo is very a much a nipper, but conures overall can get kinda bitey. That being said, when you know how to read em, you can usually avoid most bites. She's cuddly, but very much on her own terms. Sometimes she's into head scritches, and sometimes she'd just rather sit up next to your arm or something and preen. We keep her in our living room, that way she has access to both a window, TV, and that's usually where atleast one of us is hanging out within eyesight. She has a habit of climbing down from her cage, less now as she's gotten older. Usually happens when someone is not in the room and she gets bored from her toys and stuff. Typically she'll make her way to the main table and climb to where I sit usually. 

She's pretty quiet, but has flocked called for people once in a while. Usually she's her loudest (and kinda scariest) is when she spots like a cat, hawk, or person (Pest control, etc) outside. That, or when you talk on the phone and she tries to figure out what your doing. Never really got her to talk, but again she isn't super loud all the time. I do sometimes get her to make kissing noises! Plus, you can teach em other tricks (I've gotten her to spin!).

video_description: The Peach Fronted Conure, often confused as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
The Hardiest bird ever....I give it 5 out of 5.

Your scores all air on cautionary sides.....mine is 18yrs old and I live in Wales...temperature changes are dramatic.

This bird is like owning a dog, its attention demanding for sure, and it needs it.

The Peach is the king of conures, very smart, very reading...its not the most playful bird, it's more reserved than most conures, but they do like attention.

They display their mood very expressively through their body , wing raises, wing stretches , hisses and chest bump attacks.....but all, are mainly bravado. It's a forgiving bird.....but still susceptible to it's natural instincts on occasion.

Under scored heavily i reckon

video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content:
First off, i wish you were more north so we could grab dinner but wanted to share my piece also i have 5 birds, two green cheeks, two quakers and one lovebird. Both quakers and my second conure were given up and i worked at a bird store at the time (I dont really condone purchasing birds anymore because of actually seeing it, but that's another story). The first conure i brought home was about six months old, now about 3.5 years, its crazy how time passes and she is truly my spirit animal, understands me more than any person. My lovebird never really truly bonded with me but does step up. My green quaker is older at about 10 years old, does not fly, and has only bonded with a family member but it's okay. My blue quaker is pretty sociable with everybody and is definitely the most playful..

And here's my problem child,  my pineapple conure was a rehome and he's about estimated 6/7 years old.. i do not really know his previous situation which i know can complicate things. But he does not like hands at all,  he will hang out on my shoulder and does preen me, never have gotten bitten by him ever. But anybody else, he still does not like hands but he actually really goes to attack anybody else. All this to say i know he does trust me and if i approach very slowly he will let me pet his neck, but very infrequently.. any suggestions on how to work with him to be more acceptable with handling/ hands?

video_title: Why Parrots May Bond to One Person (And What You Can Do About It)
video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content: So what happens if you never touched your bird inappropriately? I brought home a 7 year old, male Quaker Parrot a year ago. From day one he feigned regurgitation for my fingers and tried to mate with my hands.  I hadn't ever scratched him yet on the head let alone anywhere else. I had researched parrots before getting one so I knew not to rub under the wing and such.  I had only ever had him on my shoulder and perched on my finger. I met him once before to see how he liked me. I spent 3 hours with him before coming back a week later to pick him up.  The first time i met him he flew onto my shoulder, stared me down, gave a head bop and proceeded to rub his beak and face into my beard. I thought he was just friendly but now I suspect he was courting me.  Since bringing him home he frequently gets "excited", even if I just talk to him and even just a short amount of head scratches. I had tried various diets, extra sleep. Attempt to discourage the behavior. I never let him mate with my hand and I will pull away, set him down or put him in the cage but nothing has ever worked.

video_title: Mexican Red Crowned Amazon, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Red Headed Amazon, aka Green Cheeked Amazon, is an insanely fun big personality parrot but the question is, is this the ...
text_content:
What a lovely, insightful video :)

My grandmother got an Amazon back in the eighties and as you might imagine, back then, no one knew how to take care of such a bird. What little information there was, mostly was aimed to make the owners life easier, not ensure the parrot is kept properly. So, after being caught in the wild as a baby, shipped around the world, he spent his first decade in a way too tiny cage, mostly being ignored, because he wasn't handleable and became more and more aggressive over time.
As soon as I was old enough to realize, I tried and succeeded to bond with him. But being a kid still, I too didn't fully understand how to make his life better. Over time, I learned and tried my best to improve his situation, while he still lived with my granny. One day I finally managed to take him with me.
He now has a huge enclosure and since I work from home, he is with me most of the time. I am glad to be able to keep him near me all the time, since finding a bird friend for him was deemed impossible by several experts - he is absolutely terrified of other birds, probably due to what happened during the transport. Alas, he is enjoying attention and love as much as appropriate food. While I try my very best to make his birdy life an awesome one, we still have to work through a lot of issues due to all the years of neglect. But he is doing a great job and is a wonderful friend and I see that he is happy now.
All that being said, I still feel guilty for not helping him sooner, but I will continue to make it up to him.

Seeing you and your peers doing your best to educate people about those animals is just wonderful and I am sure you are saving many of them from ending up in awful situations. Thank you!

text_content: I have one for 10 years so far.  He is feisty, even though we had him as a baby bird.  I’m the only one that can handle him.  He hates my uniform- he will try to bite hard if I put my hand in his large flight cage wearing my uniform.  He loves listening and copying my hubby, but will bite him if he had the chance.   He calls out my kids names all day if he hasn’t seen them.  He copies a few bird calls, crow, seagull mainly.  He loves to listen to other bird chatters.  Wild parrots have landed on his cage.  He goes on a screeching rampage every morning and evening.  We give him walnuts to try to quiet him at those times.  He bows his head for scratches, he fluffs his feathers when asked to shake, open his wings on command, flaps his wings holding onto perch when I say let’s fly.  He also says rawr, I’m a lion.  You know when he will be mischievous when his eyes pin and he stretches himself out,  and fluffs.  The big give away is the evil laugh-  he definitely goes on the defensive when strangers appear. video_title: Amazon parrot gets a beak makeover #petbirds #birds #beaktrim
text_content:
I was once given an older Amazon with a similar beak problem; only worse!  He, too, had been raised on a diet primarily of seed, and his beak was so severely overgrown that my Vet said he couldn't trim it back, because he was afraid his nerves may have been involved. 
So, I provided him with a cuddle bone, rock, stick, and put sandpaper on 1 of the perches in his cage, and also attempted to gradually change his diet to a healthier one (as recommended by my Vet), but he didn't use them, and refused to eat. It was sad. He was a miserable, unhappy boy. Soooooo unlike most parrots that we're used to seeing. 

This video was very informative, and I believe he may have died from liver failure. 

I think a lot of people just sincerely don't know that the bird seed that is sold in Pet Stores would be harmful to their birds.

video_description: Tips for new bird owners coming in hot. ✨ Birds are social creatures craving connection and companionship. Set aside ...
text_content: Many times I've been bothered by the fact that other friends (and even people) have larger, "more difficult" parrots they keep just fine, without any experience  and tell me they're easy, while I have 1 lovebird and find him challenging.  That doesn't match up. Later on I realized parrots aren't difficult  to own. They're difficult to DO RIGHT and just "owning one" doesn't make a success story. It's be easy for anyone to just own a bird - clean it, feed it... if they don't work with it. My friends African grey is scared of everything so she doesn't do much with him. But an inactive, fearful parrot is not a success even if it doesn't cause issues. So it's important  to note that, yes many owners "cope" but their birds intelligence is unused and unsatisfied. I find my lovebird challenging because I try to keep it mentally stimulated, well-trained, which he is. I can do most things with him my friends can't do with their own parrots.

Hey there Blue Plant Pets! I just wanted to ask for some help with my bird, since you know a lot about many of them.

And although I’ve learned a lot more about taking care of them over the next few years, all the videos or questions or searches have been about first getting birds, not having them for a while.
So, this is the problem I hope you could help me fix. Ari does not let me touch her, let me hold her, or perch on my finger. She most she’ll let me do is hold my finger out for her to gently touch her beak.
Ari has been my friend for years, and I just want to make sure that eventually I’ll be able to show her that I won’t hurt her.

Please and thank you. ❤❤❤

P.S., I am probably going to get a new bird in hopes that if I train the new bird into actually liking me, maybe Ari can learn to like me as well. I don’t know if I will be able to, though.

A song of bliss.
Oh yeah, my cats give them a wide berth. Those big ol' beaks...

video_description: These are super popular parrots BUT they may not be good for beginners except for the absolute bravest of people willing to put ...
text_content:
I had/have three of the parrots on this list. 
My first parrot was a sulphur crested cockatoo, which I got as a kid. She was my beginner bird, & she was not hard to look after. I see cockatoos often mentioned in lists of parrots that are not ideal beginner birds, & yet, mine was the easiest of all my birds to look after. Noise was not an issue. She sometimes squawked in the evenings, but a cocky squawk is not shrill or abrasive like some other parrots. 
I presently have an Amazon & eclectus. The Amazon is not difficult to look after. Even though he’s loud, he’s not noisy. He likes spending time with me but is also happy to chill out on his own. 
I’d agree that eclectus are high maintenance. I wouldn’t recommend one as a first, second or third parrot. Diet is a big deal with mine. I can’t feed him pellets because they make him toe tap. Unlike the eclectus on this list, mine is noisy & wants the most attention. On the positive side, he’s got a sweet personality & has never bitten anyone or me (whereas the Amazon bites). My eclectus has a larger than life personality & loves showing off in front of people. But his excitable nature makes him noisy, & most of his noises are shrill & high pitched.

text_content:
Back when I was young, I found a turtle in a coulee. Took him, put him in a bucket with a rock. I fed him bugs I found in the yard or around the house. Maybe some lettuce or cabbage. Even threw in a small lizard or two. Didnt know they actually ate lizards. worms were his favorite. But he usually hated beetles because everytime he ate one, he'd spit it out. 

Id clean out his bucket and his shell when they became too mossy. Just used regular hose water. We kept him outside all year long, even when it was cold. My parents didnt want a big old bucket of stinky water in the house.

I was scared when I walked outside to see the top of the bucket water completely frozen over. Thought my turtle had froze to death. But once the ice thawed, he was alive and well every time. That must have something to do with turtles being cold blooded.

But one day it rained and the bucket overflowed with water and thats when the turtle got out and we never saw him again. We think he made it back to the coulee.

Thats how I took cared for my turtles when I was a kid. But now that Im older and my nieces and nephews want a pet turtle, Im looking for more modern ways of taking care of turles. Even though im sure the traditional methods works, I just dont want their bucket to overflow with water and the turtle manages to get out.

text_content: I’ve been herping for many years and the Bog (or Muhlenberg’s) Turtle is the ONLY species native to the Tri-State region (NY, NJ, CT) that I’ve never seen in their native habitat. I have only seen adults and hatchlings in one place; the Stony Brook Lab & Fish Hatchery on Long Island, NY. They have several pair in a breeding program because they are a critically endangered species on CITES. Their small size made them ideal pets and during the early 1900s they were over collected for the pet trade. This in combination with habitat destruction led to their near extinction in the wild.

text_content: When I was a kid, my buddy lived on a farm with some pretty big ponds on the property. The biggest pond had swans and all of a sudden they started losing feet. Turns out there was a 4ft wide, 5ft long alligator snapper in the pond. We used to swim and fish in that pond all the time. When my buddy's mom finally got a picture of the snapper up on the bank, I got chills knowing I was swimming with that thing. Noone ever swam in that pond again lol. Nope, nope, and nope. I've moved smaller ones out of the road here and there and when they snap it sounds like a trap snapping shut. Your finger would be like a toothpick. Also be careful because these sobs have long necks. Just when you think you're out of bite range, you're not. They're amazing animals that have survived forever. Looking at one up close is like looking back in time, like a living dinosaur.

text_content:
My husband and I have spent 2 spring/summer seasons rescuing turtles off the roads in our area. We have saved around 30 turtles of differing species and have even had to help some of them heal from injuries and malnutrition before releasing them back into the wild near where we found them, though we made sure they were a good distance from the road when releasing them. 

I am proud to say we have helped these amazing creatures and hope there are others doing similar things in their areas as well. Just stopping to help a turtle get across the road safely is a good thing to do to help lessen the number of injured or killed turtles. All it takes is a few minutes of your time and a small amount of effort on our parts. 

Thank you for another great video Clint. Can’t wait for the next one. ☺️💕

text_content:
Clint ,
I grew up (age 2 to 18) on a 27 acre farm in Jackson, N.J. , bordered on the south side by a brook(the north branch of the Metedeconk River.
Across the road from our Victorian farmhouse was another old farm with a house dating back to the mid 18th century.
It had been farmed by the Clayton family for many generations and many generations of children had played with Box Turtles which frequented the wild strawberries growing  there.
It wasn't uncommon to find turtles with "plastrons". with initials on them carved there by these children.
In the late fifties, I was visiting the field and found a huge box turtle.
On it's plastron was carved,  "Lt. Opey Y Co. 189?
I took a piece of brown paper and copied the carving just as it was on the turtle.
 I almost brought it home, almost! It had survived for so long I did not want to endanger it in any way.
I never researched him, but Lt. OPey may have been a Spanish American or possibly a First World War Veteran.
So, I've always wondered jut how old was that Turtle(Terrapin) to have had that much carving still very readable,
when Opey left his message?
I'll never know,  but  that was the most amazing Terrapin encounter I ever had.
Cheers,
Rik Spector

text_content: Daughter found an injured box turtle about a month ago.  She looks like something tried to eat it.  The left eye is missing and has a crack in her shell.  She took it somewhere and was told that the eye was infected.  I tried to bring it back to health by giving it some penicillen.  It wasn't able to open it's mouth.  After a week with medicine, it was able to eat the grounded up bugs/worms that I made mush out of.  the next week, she was eating lettuce, carrots and some fruits.  Hopefully she will be able to eat live worms and more soon.  I got her to my home and she now has a great home that PetSmart and another turtle owner helped me set up.  I am afraid that she will never be able to be returned to the wild, but I take her outside everyday so that she can feel the grass and wind. Love my new pet.

text_content: I’ve had my EBT for 30yrs now. I was 5yrs old in ‘91 when I found her wandering through our milk parlor on our dairy farm of all places & since at age 5 I was already in love with TMNT I picked her  up bc I didn’t want her to get stepped on by a cow first of all but also bc I wanted a pet turtle lol. She’s never been a shy or scared turtle at all, in fact she’s never completely “boxed up” on me that I can ever remember. Idk for sure an age and my vet isn’t totally sure either but I did learn she was a she when we first took her to a vet when I first found her. I built a big “tortoise table” for her about 17-18yrs ago when I was at college & took her with me to my apartment and she’s loved it ever since. I have a substrate side for her on 1 end with both a heat pad & light, in the middle I transitioned to a small soil area where there’s ferns, dandelions, & lilacs that she eats right out of the ground as the grow with the help of a light & misting system and then on the far side I have a sloping transition to a basking pool. Her table is 8ftlong by 3ft wide with 6inch sides around the whole frame so she doesn’t see through, walk off and hurt or kill herself by cracking or shattering her shell. This is a really great video for anyone who has an EBT they wanna improve their husbandry skills with or are thinking of getting one & doing their due diligence beforehand which I highly recommend with ANY animal you plan on getting as a pet!

video_title: My Box Turtle Care Guide! How I Take Care Of My Oldest Pet (Learn From My Mistakes)
video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content: I was given a 3 toed box turtle 35 years ago by a coworker whose daughter was abusing it. She told me it ate wet Purina Cat Chow. I found that even if I put the food out every day, it only seemed interested about once a week so I only put out food once a week after that. Also, I found that since it has free range of the house, it can still sense Summer and Winter and it sort of hibernates in the Winter and doesn't eat. I still have a 2" deep water dish out at all times if it wakes up and needs to hydrate. I had read that the red eyed ones were males and brown eyed ones were females. I got confirmation of that when one day while the food was out and it was basking under the lamp that I put over the food dish. He was "fanning". His junk was all hanging out of his cloaca. It scared the shit out of me but when I got close to check it out, he sucked it all back in. Let me tell ya, there's an awful lot of junk in that trunk. A little research told me what was going on there. Considering what our life is like here, I'd say it is the easiest pet I have ever had. Even in the Summer, I could be gone for a week and no harm done. After 35 years, I must be doing something right. He was full grown size when I got him so I have no idea how old he is. He could drop dread tomorrow for all I know. I may need to get his beak trimmed. It's getting quite long but he can still eat the Cat Chow with no problem.

text_content: I'm holding my box turtle right now! I've had her for 35 YEARS!  When my nephew was seven, he found and kept her. She was sick and wouldn't eat, so I nursed her back to health and asked if I could have her. Box turtles are a big responsibility. Temperature is very important. If they don't like their conditions, they go on hunger strikes, get weak and die! They need exercise, interaction, and a big aquarium. They are expensive! Canned food, eye drops, shell lotion, heat lamps, reptile bark, special bulbs, big aquariums, vet bills ,etc.  So leave them in the wild or you might have a dead turtle and a sad kid.                                                          That being said ...I love my turtle, and she loves me. She fusses for me to hold her. Plays tug of war and bite the beard.  Likes classic rock music, swims in a big pot for exercise on bath day. I try to hold her for a few hrs every day. Her name is'' Cordelia'' but mainly call her ''Turtle Pants''. A fun pet but not an easy one!                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    PS. .... If you already have a box turtle, I recommend Zoomed box turtle canned food. It's about $3.50 a can (4 feedings) and a steady feeding schedule. My turtle eats every 2 1/2 days.  Mine won't eat dry food. Lettuce doesn't have enough nutrition and bananas is like giving them candy every day. Special box turtle food has fruits, vegetables, fish meal, vitamins, proteins etc.  Also, a bulb that gives both UVA and UVB rays about $30 to $60. Bulbs give them vitamins, so does the eye drops and lotion.  also a aquarium thermometer. and carefully trim the beak and toenails.

text_content: I have moved multiple box turtles off the road in the direction they were facing- or if that direction looks dangerous, into nearby woods. Recently I stopped for a 6" male that had a 1" piece of shell broken off and hanging. The wound was dirty and festering a little. He was on a super steep dangerous part of Hwy 52 so I brought him home, cleaned and treated the wound, and glued the shell shard back in place with cyanoacrylate. That held nicely, and I am monitoring him to ensure infection doesnt grow. I made him a 6' outdoor pen with food, water, shade, shelter, and plants. He sure sleeps a lot, and I have not observed him eating, so maybe he is sad, or in pain, or both. Soon as the wound heals fully I will release him (back near where I found him if I can find a safe spot). Thanks for an excellent video!

text_content: Fabulous critter! When I was a kid in Northern Virginia in the early 60s, we would see them all the time when we were exploring in the woods. I made a "farm" for them in our backyard to save as many as I could from habitat (and of course their direct) destruction by graders for new housing developments nearby. When we had to move due to my father's reassignment, we relocated our "turtles" (tortoises) to less threatened areas. Interesting factoid, at least in my experience, if you face them into a tree and go off and do your other adventures in the woods, including finding more of them, and come back they'll still be there faced into the tree! I don't think they have much of a sense of reverse! As this video creator suggested, very gentle and even affectionate souls. Tide for my favorite reptile... the other discovered in our eventual move to the Southwest being the desert horned lizard, aka "horny toad"-- same thing, character-ful, gentle souls.

text_content: I had a snapping turtle that was rescued from our parking lot on a hot summer day as a fresh hatchling, about the size of a small coin.  I raised her and handled her every day for years.  Her and I would go all over.  She got so used to people that she was like a puppy, with Petsmart being one of her favorite places.  Usually trying to climb on my shoulder and then from a shopping cart (with padding) when she got too big, trying to get a look at everything.  I had so much trust in her that even when she was big I would try to give her as much free roam time around the house as she wanted.  She usually ended up exploring some then coming back to me, try and dig into my side a bit and fall asleep.  Saying that though, I would never recommend one as a pet to anybody.  Mine was a sweety but it took me years of spending hours with her every day for her to become tame.  And all it takes is 1 bite to mess you up pretty bad for life.

text_content:
Aggression in male box turtles is very common particularly after emerging from estivation in April (up north.) It’s actually desirable to let many species interact aggressively as they would in the wild just before breeding. I’d allow both my Eastern Box and Spotteds to have at it (under supervision) before mating as it elevates their testosterone levels and stimulates their breeding response. I’ve noticed higher levels of aggression if a female of their species is present (a “show of strength?”) Also, there seems to be a direct correlation with color; the more brightly colored/patterned the male (like Otis) the more dominant/aggressive the turtle. It would be interesting if Chris could breed Otis to see if any of his offspring inherit his “curiosity” traits. 
I noticed for how colorful Otis is his eyes appear to be light orange opposed to the more typical brilliant, fiery red of.  male Easterns. 

*Could Otis be overcompensating due to the “inferiority complex” his pale eyes have given him? In Eastern Box Turtles “eye color matters!”* 😊

text_content: I have a horsefield tortoise (or Russian tortoise for you guys in the states) who is just like Otis! If I take him out of his enclosure he will just follow me everywhere and lifts himself up and stretches his neck out as much as he can towards me, and interestingly he NEVER bites me (only my shoes) he's just interested in what I'm doing, but he will constantly try to nip at my girlfriend, so he clearly has preferences. The funny thing is that when I feed him I have to leave the room, even if he can't see me he will not eat while I'm there, he'll just scratch relentlessly against the wall of his enclosure until I pay him attention, so I can only assume he knows my scent, because I've tried just sitting quietly in the room and he still goes nuts as if he knows I'm there. I wish I fully understood why he's so obsessed with me and keen to interact, but I'm not complaining. I personally believe that it's because when we got him he was quite sick - he barely ate or moved and certainly had metabolic bone disease, his shell was pliable and he was super skinny, and I wasn't working at the time due to lockdown so I put all of my time into nursing him back to health. I hand fed him, bathed him in water treated with calcium and vitamins every day, and even gently held up his shell to help him learn how to walk properly until he was strong enough to lift it on his own, so I'm convinced that he somewhat understands that I'm the reason that he's alive and that's why he is the way he is with me.

text_content: Thank you. This is great information! We have a 10 year old captive bred Stinkpot that we adore. His shell has retained shed flakes that have built up over the past year. We had his water too warm ~79*F and since he never used the basking area we only had a UVA/UVB bulb over the water. He started coming out onto his ramp more and more and we think this was to get out of the water that was too warm. We have the water at 76*-77*F now and have a basking bulb and UVA/UVB light. We were also told in a musk turtle group that all water conditioners are toxic to turtles and we will need drain out the water in his 55 gallon setup (we have it filled about1/3 full) and to use RO or filtered water with no conditioner. We used a new extra soft toothbrush to gently work the retained flakes loose and did a 50% water change with a faucet attachment on the sink for filtered water but it took 4 hours for the water in the tank to come up to temperature because the sink water has to be cold through the filter. What are your thoughts on the safety vs risks of water conditioners such as the Tetrafauna Aquasafe turtle water conditioner or others like it? Thank you

text_content:
My family has had a loggerheaded musk turtle since I was a young kid - back then youtube didn't exist and it was insanely hard to find any information on the turtle. It's actually super-fun to see videos of the turtle and kind of heartwarming to see other turtles of the same type acting like she did when she was young.
I'm quite surprised that you can handle them without getting the ol' bitey-attempts from the turtles, though. The first few years of her life, ours tended to threateningly open her mouth whenever she was held (by the way, she was always placed on a palm, not just held aloft...), and sometimes even try hail-mary attempts to bite where-ever she thought a finger or hand might be. 

Fun fact, we got our turtle when I was 10, and I'm now now 38 years old - which means our silly old turtle-lady is far older then they're supposed to get, at an impressive 28 years old.

video_title: MY $10,000 TURTLE TANK 🐢
text_content:
This tank is massive and absolutely beautiful! Having tank that is less than 10gal/shell length inch is a very common mistake and your turtles are lucky to have tons of room to swim and tons of privacy areas to explore. 

Some suggestions I can provide as a turtle owner (can also find this on r/turtle)
1. Eventually each turtle needs their own tank. As babies, it is ok for them to be in the same tank, but they will get aggressive before fully reaching maturity. Pay attention to neck biting as a clear sign that it is time to separate them.
2. Again with the territorial behavior, it would be better to give each turtle its own basking area and lamp. Even as babies, one of them will start bullying others for the prime basking area.
3. The lamp you had was a single dome so I’m not sure if your bulb is just a heat bulb or a heat/uvb bulb. But turtles also need uvb and people have found that combination bulbs are not reliable at providing uvb. So it would be better to have a dual bulb lamp and have a dedicated uvb bulb to prevent arthritis in your turtles.
4. I saw some suggestions for a pump, but I did see some bubbling in one of your frames so it looks like you have one. Just make sure the pump is 5-10x the volume of your tank. This is another common mistake. So if your tank is 200 gal, you want a pump that can at least pump 1000gal/h. You might have to have multiple pumps in order fulfill that flowrate. 

Hope this helps! It looks like you spare no expense for your pets so I know you will be great turtle dad!

text_content: Feeding goldfish and Rosie's will eventually kill your turtles. They have a chemical called thiaminase which will build up in the turtle's system and when it gets bad it will display as neurological issues with the turtles. It blocks vitamin B12, and a heavy dose of B12 can fix it if noticed soon enough. My advice is to Google fish that don't contain thiaminase and use those, you can even buy frozen fish, thaw them out and chop them up and the turtles won't see any difference. Also I love how you did an elaborate setup for very common turtles, you're putting people to shame! Many species need different types of cages or temperatures so please keep that in mind. My side job is reptiles in general, monitor lizards and snakes (dozens of species) if you ever need more specific info hit me up. Check out Fox Reptiles if you are ever in Ohio and need a cool video with an insanely rare pretty ass monitor lizard or something. --Steve

video_title: Transforming my Tiny Turtle's Tank
video_description: Me when the turtle is turtling Music: Peanut Butter Sandwich - Tiki Tiki Urban Conspiracy - Jules Gaia Freudian Slip - Bladverk ...
text_content: Ok, out of kindness here is some turtle care needs and warnings for keeping turtles, I looked though the comments and noticed some people had outdated or incorrect turtle care recommendations and stuff. I just need people to understand this before getting turtles because I had read every comment and I do not want anyone getting turtles and unknowingly putting them at risk. And the one thing that bothered me the most in the comments is “you should get more turtles” or “Get him a friend “ like idk if you guys know but NO! 👉TURTLES👏 ARE 👏NOT 👏SOCIAL👏 ANIMALS 👏AND 👏THEY 👏WILL 👏HAVE 👏A 👏HIGH 👏POSSIBILITY 👏FOR👏 HARMING👏 IF 👏NOT 👏KILLING 👏EACH 👏OTHER! 👏IF 👏YOU 👏WANT 👏MORE👏 THEN 👏ONE 👏TURLE 👏IN👏THE👏SAME👏TANK👏THEN 👏YOU 👏WOULD 👏NEED 👏A 👏LARGER 👏TANK! 👏AND 👏EVEN 👏THEN 👏THERE 👏WOULD👏 ALWAYS 👏HAVE 👏A 👏RISK 👏OF 👏THEM👏BEING 👏HURT 👏OR 👏DYING! 👈 I’M NOT SAYING TO BE RUDE, I AM SAYING THAT TO PREVENT ANY IMPULSE BUYING THAT WOULD LEAD TO A HURT OR DEAD TURTLE AND PREVENTING A NEW OWNER FROM LEARNING THE HARD WAY THAT CAN BE HEARTBREAKING! 💔 So if you get a turtle the general rule for aquatic turtles is 10 gallons of water per inch of shell length, meaning a 4-inch turtle needs a minimum 40-gallon tank, and adults often require 75-120+ gallons; PUR TURTLE, DOUBLE THAT AMOUNT FOR TWO THOUGH I WOULD NOT RECOMMEND HOMEING THEM TOGETHER! this provides enough swimming space and accounts for their growth, but remember it's a minimum, and larger is always better. Always have a basking area that a turtle can get completely dry on and with that they need heat and UVB, preferably not the conjoined double lamp heat and UVB because the UVB light  in those kits aren’t the best, instead a tube UVB light that’s a 5T light is more recommend and make sure both the heat and UVB light hit the same basking area, also a water filter is also important and because turtles are messy, I say for example if you have one baby turtle in a 20 gallon long tank get a filter that is at least met for a 40-55 gallons (that what works for me at least with the tiny baby turtle) and as your turtle grows they will need to be upgraded so if you wanna save on money look into buying second hand tanks or using storage tubs or stock tanks, you don’t even have to fill the tank up all the way if it’s still a baby, you can start with a 75 gallon tank for example and keep it shallow and just raise the water level as it grows (keep in mind the water should be deep enough for it to swim but shallow enough to easily reach the surface to breathe, typically 1.5 to 2 times its shell length, or about 4-8 inches, gradually increasing as it grows stronger, ensuring it can always stand on its hind legs and touch the bottom or easily get to the surface.) and as for plants it depends on the turtle, I have some turtles that would ignore the plants (like my Mississippi map turtle) who would eat the romaine lettuce and sliced carrots but won’t eat the guppy grass in his tank or the hornwort. Or my baby red belly cooter who would eat every plant I give her besides the hornwort. Turns out turtles in the wild don’t care for hornwort do to the texture of the plant even if it’s safe to eat. None of my turtle eat or even like hornwort so you can try hornwort if you like just keep in mind it’s a floating plant,doesn’t need substrate. Also when it comes to substrate there is many options to choose from, you can do sand, tile, no substrate, large river rocks (make sure they are too big for the turtle to eat) but I would stay away from aquarium gravel because it can cause  impaction if eaten and there is many bad stores of turtles dying from aquarium gravel. As for decoration make sure that it’s something that would NOT crush the turtles, something that wouldn’t risk the turtle getting stuck, something that does not have blade like edges,and something that is aquarium safe that the turtle won’t eat, sometimes people use plastic plants but for me personally I wouldn’t trust it because I heard some turtles nip at it thinking it’s a real plant at first and we wouldn’t want our beloved turtles to eat plastic. Another thing is food, do NOT feed turtles  goldfish, rosy red minnows(any any minnows),and other cyprinids (like common minnows, dace) away from turtles because they contain thiaminase, an enzyme that destroys Vitamin B1, causing neurological damage, paralysis, and death in turtles, and also avoid aggressive fish like Cichlids that can harm the turtle's eyes. Slow, colorful, or long-finned fish like Bettas, Koi, or large Plecos are also bad choices as they are easy prey or attract unwanted attention, while fast, small livebearers (guppies, mollies, platies, and mosquito fish) are better options for enrichment or occasional treats, but always monitor for safety. Also turtles need a water heater, even more so in their baby and juvenile year! As for main food pellets there is many options just try and keep it diverse and try to avoid as much of the low quality ones if possible, for me personally I use Ultra Fresh Floating Turtle food, zoo med turtle food, and mazuri turtle food. Also almost forgot but make sure you had a cuddle bone for calcium,turtles need it for their shell health. ANYWAY Please make sure to do your research before getting a turtle let alone any pet, turtles are a big commitment and can live for many years! And if one day you end up not being able to care for the turtle do NOT release it into the wild, turtles brought up in captivity can not survive and if they do they can become invasive in some areas, best to responsibility find the turtle a new home if one day you decide you don’t want it or can’t care for it anymore!

text_content:
I'm not proud to say this, but I have been not taking care of my turtle at all. I've had my turtle, Froppy, for one year. I used to be really depressed, and I got him for a way of mental support, but it didn't work out so well when I found out a good filter costs 100 dollars. I bought one filter that I could say, was doing its job really well, but the problem is that my tank water was really small, the capacity was only around 7 gallons. I used to keep my 5 inch turtle in a 7 gallon tank, that was not filled all the way up because it could've easily escaped.(keep in mind I didn't have a lid). I kept my turtle in that exact same tank for one year, the water was like 2 inches tall. It was a really bad life for my turtle. 
Some months ago, I saw my turtle refuse to eat, walk around or even sit on the magnetic island that couldn't keep his weight anymore, and when he'd sit, the island would sink. I got the sign. There was something wrong about the way I was treating him. I used to not feed him, or feed him rarely. I knew I had to do something, even though I had not even close to enough money. I couldn't ask my friend for money, because they are barely surviving, getting paid one dollar per hour and I usually give them a visit and order food while I'm there. (they are disabled and no workplace would accept them) So I got a job. At starbucks. Not the best paid, but it was good enough to save around 300 dollars to get everything my turtle needs. In 2 months, I had the money. With a lot of luck, my turtle survived because I cleaned the aquarium more often now, I fed him and I gave him conditions for him to survive. 
Today was the day my turtle got the biggest present of his life. He got a 35 gallon tank, 31 inch wide and 15 inch tall. I filled it up all the way with tap water, and now I'm letting it sit for around a week(I have two reasons for that. 1: tap water is not safe,) And the second reason is that I ordered some really nice fake plants, a water filter with activated charcoal, a lid for my tank with led lights and a big island. I don't know if the tank is big enough, but it sure is better than before. My turtle will feel so much better, and probably get used to me.
I will watch more of your videos and other turtle owner content creators so I'd see how to take care of turtles.

text_content:
I am 70 yrs old and live in a mobile home park with a very large pond (football field) in the middle, well it went toxic last November ck, filled with foam and bacteria, years of poop and such, we also have a lot of ducks. Park tried to deal with, but out of their league and too cheap to hire pros, long story short is they all started dying, we had probably 100 sliders, up to 20-30 yrs old.  It was killing me watching them, die such slow deaths.  

I finally started rescuing the ones that were visibly were sick, gave them ABX shots etc. with the help of an online vet from ND (I am CA) saved 30 out of 34 and rehomed privately., Fast forward 4-5 months, came the babies.  The first round cost a lot of money and was hard on me for the 6 weeks nursing them back to health. Well, all my senior friends who walk around the pond with their dogs, first brought me 1 baby, then 2-3-4.  I kept saying please no more, I do not have the $$, capacity or strength to raise them to 4" (my heart is too sensitive) when I can adopt them out to a reptile store... BUT I can't turn them down either, they were the size of a quarter and pond still toxic.  

1 died after 4 months, I have no idea why I tested water almost daily food, basking etc. all checked. His name was Only (at the time the "only" one who made it) he was doing great and then just died on my 70th birthday, I am very sensitive to helpless creatures, and it wrecked me.  That's when the others started coming and now have 3.  

One look like she's (guessing) on steroids she's growing so fast and the other isn't growing as fast, probably male, they are in a 30-gallon tank together even though they say not to, they are best buds, the smaller rides on the bigger ones back.  Then came, 3 more recently and I had a 10 gallon that I already had bought for "Only".  I know I need bigger tanks, but I cannot afford nor have the room to go any bigger.  

Too small to adopt out in CA as it's illegal. The all of a sudden, the bigger one (Chance) was very lethargic, eyes looked swollen, (possibly upper respiratory), so I freaked out and took them both to animal ER which was a joke and "very" expensive. Then they seemed fine again, now Chance is doing it again, he won't bask, stays hanging on the water lettuce plant all day, BUT she is eating and swimming. I am at my wits end and emotionally it's killing me because I don't have the vast knowledge I need. I have a lot but they're still too small to give ABX shots safely. Every single item I read on internet, I am doing. 

If anyone can give me some advice it would be greatly appreciated.  This is a full-time job, and I am not that strong to lift tanks to completely clean, I have to get someone over to do that part. Water changes I am fine with and again water temps, food, cleanliness, etc. all CHECKED❤❤!  PLEASE HELP ME, I don't want to lose Chance, Sassy and Little one (name for now).😢😢 Thank you!

text_content: I got my Red Eared Slider Turbo when she was approx 1-2 years old, from a cousin who didn't want her anymore. That's her, my icon picture. She had to have an x-ray. I had no idea what I was in for, but I wouldn't trade her for the world. She's been with me now for 17 years, most of my life. She lives in a 110-gallon Tuff-Tub, for which I have custom-built a landing platform and heat and light rig for her. Full-spectrum + UVB + ceramic heat emitter + a canister filter rated for a 500-gallon outdoor pond. Here's what they don't tell you: when you have a turtle this big they don't make habitat supplies for you. You have to get very comfortable thinking outside the box, breaking out the power tools, and making late-night trips to your local hardware store sometimes. But I love my baby! She's smart, stubborn, the vet always complements her on her beautiful carapace, and she's afraid of precisely nothing. She's the perfect fit for me as a companion animal.

text_content:
Awesome video, as usual! I'm glad you heavily emphasized the difficulty of keeping/affording these turtles, because my male RES is by far my most expensive pet I have (and that says a lot, as I have fish and aquascaped aquariums.)  To add on a little more useful information:
- I'd definitely cycle the filter in advance (like with any fish tank, really). This will take 4-8 weeks, depending on how fast the beneficial bacteria, namely Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter, are able to establish in the biological media. This will remove all ammonia and nitrites from the water and convert them to nitrates. Ammonia can and will burn your turtle's eyes, and even skin, but nitrate is much less toxic and gets changed out with weekly water changes.
- Cohabbing turtles is pretty common, but a lot of the time it's not a good idea. You'll need a lot more space if you want to keep 2+ turtles, and you'll have to have a backup tank if things go wrong. Usually, turtles will be okay together as hatchlings and suddenly become quite aggressive as juveniles/adults, so just because they're okay together as babies does not mean they'll live happily together as adults. If you do want to house multiple adults together, you'd be best off with only females or at most one male and females, as multiple males together is too risky. You'd also need a lot more space, preferably a pond if you want 3+. Also, when cohabbing males and females there's the chance of ending up with hatchlings, which you have to deal with.
- Aquariums are expensive. So, if you don't care too much about aesthetics, I'd recommend a stock tub as they're built to hold water and are much less expensive. I got a 150 gallon from Tractor Supply for $115 for my male RES. It doesn't look as nice as a glass tank, but a glass tank of that size can run you $800-$1500 dollars.

video_title: Two headed turtle Update‼️
text_content:
Good evening.  I am so  glad your patient(s), survived the ordeal of the past few days/week.  I’ve got some gen. tips.  First, I am a retired animal keeper of a rescue zoo in PA.  Over my tenure of 30 + yrs., I’ve served as head keeper of all the depts., (domestics + exotics), mainly w/  birds, mammals and reptiles.  Anytime you put a turtle, baby or adult, in a container, please know that even a weak critter will try to escape, it gets them into real trouble as you’ve now experienced.  Set your container up to replicate nature, they will feel more that it’s their home.  We set our baby turtle tanks up w/ a  wet part and a dry part, doing that replicates nature.  Add some rocks they can scratch on, a few sticks and greens for them to eat, like duck weed, etc.  if using a plastic tank Start by scuffing
 the inner bottom of your smooth, of plastic, container, it provides traction for their nails to catch on to right themselves.  

It would be better to not house  turtles  on a plain, smooth tank bottom, they need enrichment in their habitat, like they’d have in nature, they live near and in fresh water ponds, they haul out during the day to sun themselves.  Gather together several pounds to of local dirt & clay, then use the clay  for a bottom layer, across the entire container.  Mix the dirt & clay together, to make a higher level and an even higher mound for 3;4 of the container.  It’s been our experience the 2 head turtles always want to do different things, even fight the other head, constantly flipping itself over, as you’ve seen.  Enriching the enclosure will help them from boredom and searching for a real pond.  To keep babies safe we use a piece of wood, a 1” x 6” x width of enclosure, of untreated wood, we use the board to divide the water side from the dry side, when they are unattended by humans, never leave them unattended on the water side.   We soak daily 10;15 min, several times a day.  After our babies are done soaking, swimming, etc., we put in the dividing board, to prevents accidents in case one flips another over or themselves over in the water,.  baby Turtles can drown, even in 1/4” water.   Also, turtles need plenty of Sun or, reptile light source, be careful though, if your glass tank is in front of a window, the Sun can overheat them fast, use a shade cloth if tank is in direct Sun or in front of a window, especially in Summer with Southern exposure.  

Soak them instead, but never leave them alone in any water.  At their stage offering water via dish is tricky for a  turtle with 2 heads, if you add a small water dish bury it in the dirt mound so it is is flush with the dirt level.  We use a teeny syringe, to offer water that way we can monitor each heads water intake.  Also a wet rag in a corner of tank is helpful, they can mouth it to moisture, while you are away.  Your turtle can also get water from foods you feed like crushed fish, plants, greens, damp turtle pellets, etc.  it’s a good idea to weigh your turtle weekly.  Good luck to you on this journey, the 2 headed turtle can be difficult to manage.   If you need help, there’s many online success stories, don’t hesitate to reach out to the folks keeping duel headed turtles alive and thriving.

text_content: Story time for anybody interested:I saved a turtle's life, so 45 minutes ago, my sister was riding her scooter around the street and she found a very small turtle, on the side of the road, and for context, the nearest pond is a 45 minute walk there, it was roughly the size of the palm of my hand, we had a bunch of cats so I didn't want to risk the cats messing with the poor lil guy, so me and my sister went to the pond near the park and as I held it, I think it started to get used to me as it was moving slowly, when I set it down near the water, it took it a few moments before it went into the water, burying under some seagrass, still trying to process that incredible moment, I'm not sure if it came from the pond of if it was someones pet they either lost, hopefully not abandoned, but I will forever cherish that moment.

text_content:
Just saying but I think you should get angonoka, Pancake, Bolson, homes hinge back, impressed, leopard and spider tortoises and these include some of my favorites along with others and the angonoka belongs to the same genus as the raidiated tortoise. 
But the pancake tortoise is one of my favorites and they are called pancake tortoises because they’re name refers to the flat shape of its shell. 
The Bolsons tortoise is a kind of tortoise that lives in North America and out of the six species of North American tortoises it is the largest with a carapace length of about 46 centimeters(18 inches).
The Home’s Hinge back tortoise is a kind of tortoise from Africa and it’s named in honor of English surgeon and naturalist Sir Everard Home 1st baronet and Sir Everard Home is best known for  first describing a large extinct marine reptile called an Ichthyosaur in the first scientific publication.
The Impressed tortoise is a relative of the Burmese mountain tortoise but the males are smaller.
The Spider tortoise is a kind of tortoise found on the island of Madagascar and they are called Spider tortoises not because they look like spiders but because of their web like pattern of yellow lines on its dark brown or black carapace or its shell. 
The last kind of tortoise I think you should get is called a leopard tortoise and it’s a large and attractively marked tortoise that can be found in the savannas of eastern and Southern Africa and it’s a grazing kind of tortoise and in the wild when the weather gets very cold or very hot it will dwell in abandoned fox, jackal and aardvark burrows and they are called leopard tortoises because of the leopard like rosettes on their shell. 
And just saying but if you do get the pancake tortoise I think that you’re son cash would like it because of how small they are but also because of how fast they are and they are also an agile climber and they are rarely found far from rocky homes and they are found not far from their rocky homes because if disturbed they can make a dash for the nearest rock crevice and since they can be easily torn apart by predators it relies on its speed and flexibility to escape from dangerous situations rather than withdrawing into its shell and the pancake tortoise is also unique in that it exploits and takes advantage of the narrow rock crevices which no other tortoise can use.

text_content: I recently rescued a russian tortoise. video_title: DO NOT GET A RUSSIAN TORTOISE! 5 SURPRISING Care Elements You Are Not Ready For
video_description: Despite how popular Russian Tortoises have become, there has not been the same return in care information as other popular ...
text_content:
For any one living in the UK watching this, our equivalent to collard greens is called spring greens. Took me weeks of searching supermarkets, small independent fruit and veg shops, market stalls and trying to find other tortoise owners to ask before I found it. Collard greens don't grow here, but spring greens are basically identical and can be used as a staple part of their diet - Morrison's do a big bag of the stuff for about £1. Also endives are usually called chicory over here.

Just want to add as well that one of the best things you can feed them is just generic garden weeds like dandelion (mostly the leaves, the flower is good for them but only about one a week) - just make sure that they don't have any pesticides or weed-killer on them.
