Most <1m pythons are great for beginners because they don’t see you as food. Pythons usually use a wrap and strangle method to kill prey, and that means if they don’t see you as viable food, they won’t try to hurt you for the most part. 

Just remember, snakes have their own temperament, just like any other animal. If your snake prefers not to be handled very much or something like that, don’t torture it. I know snake owners who don’t ever interact with their snake other than to feed and change water because the snake gets stressed out. Pythons are known as big one brain cell sweeties, but not all of them are comfortable with always being around you or out of the enclosure much.

text_content: I used to be be TERRIFIED of snakes. Still can be if they aren’t a pet and even then. My high school had a teacher that had snakes and insects and such in his classroom (I think he was a science teacher so it worked) and let students pet/ hold the non-dangerous ones so that they wouldn’t be scared of the snakes anymore. Anyway he’d just found this BRIGHT orange corn snake in the woods by his house and took it in. He said it had to have been dumped because that color would make it a target in the wild. My friend suggested I hold the snake and I did. I was terrified at first but then she was just kind of chilling on me and slowly climbing up my arm. It took a lot of the fear away because she was acting friendly and silly. She tried to get into my shirt sleeve lol. I think I’m fine with it if they are harmless snakes that are pets. In the wild I will still have a freak out about a snake though.

video_description: Little girl and her 16-foot python have been best friends since they were both SO little and tiny You can keep up with Cher, ...
text_content:
Growing up, my friend had a ball python (not as large as this snake, but still pretty big). We were taught how to gently pet him (two fingers only! not near his head!). This helped desensitize me to snakes and a young age, and I have never had any fear of them. 

Of course, I also have a healthy respect from them and will keep a wide berth from the dangerous ones. I grew up in a desert, so rattlesnakes are common in my area. I also learned that just because my friend's pet is tame, doesn't mean they all are!

text_content:
I used to have a 14ft Burmese that I got when he was around 10ft long. It was during the winter (he was a birthday gift) and the reptile store we got him from was going out of business because of the horrible way they ran the store. It was *freezing* in that store, and the snake was only like 150 dollars, which for a 10ft Burmese is mindblowing. But they needed to sell him and most others were intimidated by his size. I had them take him out of the enclosure he was in and he seemed fairly calm, but I learned a few days later it was only because he was too cold to show his aggression. For about 3 months he'd strike at his enclosure when we walked by, we had to cover his head to change his water and clean the pen, and feeding him was a lesson in stress.

Then I started letting him out of his pen and it was like night and day. The more he was out and socialized, the better he got at being handled and around people in general. Eventually he got to where he would actively seek out company with us, he'd get into the shower with us, he *loved* attention from the neighborhood kids when we'd take him out to get direct sunlight during the spring and summer. He never once even hissed at the kids, let alone tried to bite them. He basically became a giant scaly dog. People think reptiles incapable of any kind of emotional connection but they really aren't these cold heartless unfeeling machines most people believe them to be.

text_content: This is the cutest video ever omg. I think it’s so important when parents teach their young kids to both respect and love animals that’s aren’t just mammals. This girl will never have the typical bias or unwarranted hatred towards snakes that so many ppl do and learning to build a bond with a creature as different from us as a snake will make her more empathetic towards ALL creatures later in life. Snakes get so much hate that just isn’t deserved and while I understand phobias aren’t always rational, most snakes (at least here in the US) are relatively harmless. Even in regards to wild snakes(both venomous and non venomous), as long as you respect the animal via leaving it be and keeping your distance, it won’t usually harm you. Domestic snakes are generally kept as pets BC of their docile nature and you could do a lot more harm to them than the other way around. Kids are far more likely to be attacked by a dog than a pet snake and dogs are also able to cause way more severe injuries than a snake of this size (where the largest risk is simply infection). Yet bc of this stigma against them SO many snakes are murdered or even tortured by humans every day when the snake is simply minding its own business and wants to be left alone. We, as a society, need to learn to stop fearing or hating anything that’s different from us (both people and other animals) and teaching them at a young age to have compassion for an animal so misunderstood as a snake is the best way to do it imo. I think this video also shows the incredible bond snakes can have with humans despite us being so different and that they’re capable of love as well 💗

text_content:
I absolutely love berms, the ones I've known who have been handled were all sweethearts, and I've helped people get pretty decent sized ones (15ish foot was the largest berm) get used to being held. Even if they start off highly defensive (more helpful to keep in mind when a snake strikes it's a defensive act rather than aggressive one in most cases unless they honestly think your body part is food), I've found with daily work and proper care they tend to chill out relatively quickly.

The hardest one I helped a friend work with was her slightly larger reticulated python her son never touched then abandoned with her. She couldn't handle him but wanted to do her best to keep him 'cause she felt it wasn't his fault. His enclosure wasn't the best size, but once he calmed down he was usually chilling outside it.

I may love the actual giant snakes, but after a certain point size becomes the limiter, but it isn't just for size and danger level the way many think, after a certain size their feed animals get tricky. I mean- you can go to meat markets and farms to get prekilled animals telling them not to butcher the meat for you, but it gets tricky depending on where you live... even when they don't eat nearly as often as smaller snakes.

I wouldn't want to get an animal who need to eat feed animals larger than a rat, ideally frozen/thawed- and I feel bad about that 'cause I love rats as pets... unless it was fish. Far easier to go to a fish market to get whole fish of varying sizes if you're anywhere near port cities. SO easy.

So yeah- I'd sooner have an adult alligator where they can eat a wider range of meats and I wouldn't have to feed whole mammals and whole chickens consistently- though as any other reptile they need a varied diet, than a reticulated python- but to be fair, I wouldn't have alligators either.


Though I have known someone with a young alligator and "he" was awesome- so the temptation when I was told one could be gotten for me if I wanted was greater than when I was offered a breeding pair of anacondas for free.

Though won't lie- was actually tempted at the idea of them, my brain went to turning my basement apartment into a giant snake room with the sizable bedroom turned into a pool with a waterfall for them... then I thought "Nah- I really shouldn't own anything that could kill me..." and turned them down.


Edited to add- yeah- that part with you telling people what to do is needed if you aren't with people who know- when I helped with larger snakes where it goes into "I may not die but my ribs are breaking if not other bones..." mode and up I just have an open bottle of Listerine or a strong drinking alcohol around in case something happened and I get incapacitated and seriously have seconds to get it off- I think what people forget or don't think of is every time you breathe out they constrict more, so when you try to breathe in again you can't and so on until... ugh- I hate the idea of hurting a snake, but alcohol is one of the "easier" ways with a high likelyhood of success of getting a snake disengaged- that and grabbing the tail and pulling back can make continued constricting much harder for them, if not impossible- depending on the size.

Very very intentionally I've topped out my handling of the large snakes at 15 feet, I want 2 people with me at a min, esp if I know they're quirky- the alcohol of the non-rubbing kind uncapped and ready for someone to grab, and a phone close enough so 911 can be called. Only a few times I only had 1 other person, and a few times whoever was with me wasn't experienced. I have no interested with working with larger on any kind of regular basis...

I have a true story about a snake. I'm italian so this will be hard for me to tell in english, but I'll try. 
My beloved grandfather fought in ww2, but he was captured and he sadly had to spent seven years of his life as a prisoner of war in South Africa. They were in a camp and a certain day they woke up and they were told that they had to move. He had an old folded piece of fabric as a pillow and when he lifted it up he found out there was a viper under it. He had probably spent the night with this lethal snake under his head. Everybody wanted to kill the animal, but my grandaddy told them that they should let him live and go, just like the snake did with him... I was a little girl but I remember how proud of him I was... still miss him every day.

text_content: I have kept king cobras, retics, and Burmese pythons, and will have to agree on the first two's intelligence level. Kings don't go into spastic mode like many other cobras or venomous snakes do. They observe and measure you , appearing to judge your intentions based on what or how you are moving. One has to keep these animals to fully understand what I am talking about here.  Retics, on the other hand, are inquisitive; sometimes when you open up their cage and see their heads turning and tongue flicking in your direction, you'd think that a bite would be forthcoming once you pick them up, as that is what usually happens with a lot of other snakes. You don't really see that behavior in Burms unless they are anticipating food.  Also with the Burmese, you can start picking them up immediately upon opening the enclosure, but with most retics, you will have to feel them first and try to gauge their mood before reaching out.  Not saying that retics are more predisposed to aggression, it's just that they go through mood swings far more frequently than the Burmese.

video_description: Join us as we explore the top 5 best snakes for beginners, ranked from worst to best. Starting with the colorful corn snake, ...
text_content: I wish more people would give Carpet Pythons the time of day.  Irian Jaya Carpet pythons are basically as hardy as a corn snake and top out around 6ft. That was my wifes 1st snake 9 years ago and now she keeps retics and all sorts of stuff. Anyways, IJ carpet pythons have surprisingly low humidity requirements compared to other members of Morelia. They also do not need it particularly hot. Low to Mid 80s is plenty for the warm side. Unlike some carpet python localities they only really need to be misted if they are shedding. My male hasnt skipped a meal in 9 years. One of the easiest snakes i own. Even my ball python technically takes more work because i have to mist her and she likes to skip meals. It is probably true that it is harder to get bitten by a ball python than a carpet python, but my Carpet python hasnt ever bitten anyone either. text_content:
I actually interned at a zoo for awhile that kept this species.

Only reptile I’ve met that was possibly even friendlier than a bearded dragon. Our’s LOVED people, and reminded me of a giant puppy. Would follow you around the enclosure when you cleaned it in the hope you had food. Never tried to bite or attack anyone. Would come up to the front of the enclosure and sit there for hours because it liked to watch all the guests- and most of our mammals didn’t even do that. 

Wicked smart, one of the keepers did target training with it to make moving it/vet visits easier, and to keep it’s brain occupied.

BUT due to their enormous size there’s very few people I would ever recommend this to.
It’s one of those animals that basically needs it’s own room in your house, and honestly even that likely isn’t big enough. And that’s being conservative on space, not being one of those people that recommend huge enclosures for any random animal. Certain people/organizations can properly keep them and should be allowed to do so, but I wouldn’t casually recommend them to most people.

text_content: I had one of these in high school in the '70's.  I researched these as best I could before buying it, even to the point that I learned V. salvator was far superior to V. niloticus as pets.  Visited a biker in Fla. who had a 6 footer that he kept like a dog in an indoor kennel...spiked collar and all.  His lizard would come when called like a dog, and seemingly enjoyed being petted like a dog.  After that, I was hooked, and bought one a little bigger than the one in the video.  My dad built his enclosure.  He was literally like a cross between a dog, a cat and a dragon.  I enjoyed being his buddy for several years before he developed a respiratory infection.  In force-feeding him his medicine for that, I traumatized his mouth, resulting in his getting mouth rot which proved fatal.  This video brought tears to my eyes, remembering both Godzilla and my dad who helped me care for him.  Thank you.

text_content:
Here’s some random-ish facts about the Asian water monitor (and monitors in general.)
 Monitors have forked tongues, making them the only reptiles other than snakes to possess this characteristic. Like snakes, this tongue shape allows for better accuracy in locating a prey's scent.
Monitors practice 'open pursuit' hunting instead of stalking and ambushing. They are very fast despite their massive size because of their powerful leg muscles. When escaping predators, usually large snakes, water monitors will climb a tree using their powerful legs. If this technique doesn't work, they will jump from the branch into the safety of a stream or river.
Monitors are known to use their tail (which may be twice as long as its body) as a rudder (used to steer when swimming), for grasping, and as a weapon.
Monitors swallow their food whole or in large pieces; they are able to dislocate their thyroid bone in order to enlarge their throat.
Males are extremely territorial. Upon encountering another male they will first take a threatening posture then begin fighting viciously, often leaving severe bite wounds.
These monitors build their burrows along riverbanks and will take advantage of termite mounds as well as rotting logs or stumps to lay their eggs.
Asian water monitors can remain under water for up to 30 minutes - which comes in handy when hunting aquatic prey.
These water monitors are comfortable in both fresh and saltwater and have been spotted swimming far out in the ocean.
You should do a vid on draco lizards, which are just tiny dragons.

There was a trench for gas pipes that was open awaiting the pipe, each day this massive Argus would drop into the trench and do his shopping, eating every other animal in there from giant burrowing cockroaches to  skinks, elapid snakes and white's tree frogs. The tracks in the sand at the bottom of the trench showed every pursuit and capture. I eventually managed to catch that monitor one day, hauled him out of the trench and was attacked several times when I released him in the bush. By far the craziest monitor I have handled.

video_title: Savannah Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard?
video_description: The savannah monitor (Varanus exanthematicus) is one of the coolest animals on planet Earth, but does that make it a good pet?
text_content:
Absolutely love this video. Wish it came with every half assed care sheet pet stores hand off for these guys. Wouldn't give mine up for the world, but they (as many other monitors) are not what most people tend to expect with a reptile. All my monitors have rooms, Colonies of food and deep substrate. I gotta tell ya, the cost of the correct substrate as well as the cost of all the larger roach species (well over 1000$ for 8 species of medium and large roaches alone) in the past few months from private breeders, cheaper that way) for food SEVERELY outweighed the cost of converting a bedroom, vet checkups, and continuous electricity for lighting plus the lighting itself altogether. 

Almost all of my monitors, and my Argentine tegu, are adoptees/rescues from people. People who had them in a breeder (40g) tank when they were at least 2 feet long, or loving families who just couldn't take care of them anymore due to either health or housing problems (how I got my Savannahs and my tegu). They were incredibly aggressive, mean, and dangerous animals. But you give them the space they deserve, and the respect they absolutely deserve- they come around so fast it's amazing. My Nile monitor, I got him from someone who said he does nothing but bite and escape. He has the largest room in my enclosure, and does have his days where he doesn't really want to deal with me, but he immediately lost his aggression- he grew curious of me rather than fearful of me. You work on their terms, you give them respect, and in return they give you coexistence. 

Not a lovely dovey animal, definitely dangerous animals  if your not aware of how to interact with them, but incredibly intelligent and absolutely amazing to live with and I love them more than anything in the world. 

Ps. Your also correct, my arms are always scratched to hell. I do have leather handling gloves and cut proof sleeves from work (I work with razor sharp sheet metal, but every cut on me is from one of my animals) but I'm either too lazy or too forgetful to ever put them on. Lucky me I have a high pain tolerance, and but man they make you bleed a LOT.

video_title: Savannah Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard?
video_description: The savannah monitor (Varanus exanthematicus) is one of the coolest animals on planet Earth, but does that make it a good pet?
text_content:
I had one of these, impulse rescue buy from a store as she had infected feet that weren’t being treated. No idea how to keep them really (this was almost 50 years ago) She was a little smaller than this one but still pretty big. Temperament wise I got really lucky, though she only bonded to me and would hiss and threaten to tail whip visitors if she felt like it. Never hurt me in any way. She would lie across the back of the sofa behind me, her only sin was stealing fried eggs off my plate if I was eating 😄 I could hold her and she would go to sleep.
She once bit the vet that was treating her feet…somehow grabbed her finger at the first joint and refused to let go. She finally did, poor vet went to ER!
She was wild caught I’m sure. Passed away after only a year or two, feet never really properly healed and I think she had the fatty liver too. I wish I had her now, with knowledge we have. She was so intelligent and affectionate with me.
I had half a room for her enclosure but she had run of the house most of the time.

video_title: Argus Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard? + Training a Monitor Lizard With Joseph Carter the Mink Man
video_description: Argus monitors are like giant ackie monitors that love food. Ackies are great pets, but is the argus monitor a good pet as well?
text_content:
I know a guy online who keeps a breeding group of adults in a part-indoor, part-outdoor custom-built enclosure; they're killed and eaten some outdoor cats the neighbors allowed to get into their enclosure. He also has a big male named Wrath, which killed one of the several females housed with him for breeding and is generally especially aggressive and has been pulled off breeding projects.

This would probably be the monitor I'd want if I ever got enough room to keep anything bigger than an ackie. Not as impossible to find as a CB tree monitor, and I like the smaller, sleeker look on these over the big African monitors (and I doubt I'd ever have room for a croc monitor or a black-throated monitor)

text_content: I worked with monitors for decades. That was a curious tasting bite. He must have thought your neck smelled like it could have been food and he was going in for a taste. That isn’t something that can be trained out by socializing, as it wasn’t an aggressive or defensive bite. I’d never let any large monitor sit on my shoulder or be near my face, but especially a croc monitor. I know that many people put monitors on their shoulders all the time, but it only takes once for something like this to happen that leaves you with horrible and permanent consequences. It’s a miracle that you didn’t get bitten here. I’m glad that you caught this on camera and shared the video, as it is a great opportunity for you and everyone watching to see why they should be careful to not replicate this situation. Croc monitors can be great captives given the right environment and some basic safety procedures. He’s a beautiful healthy looking croc monitor. Thanks for sharing and stay safe.

text_content:
I've been saying this my whole life, from what I've seen you really can build a strong bond with most animals on this planet, assuming you were there when they were born, and they have known you for essentially their entire life.

When it comes to exotic reptiles I've only had Bearded Dragons, and I want to get a much bigger species of lizard one day, I just love lizards in particular don't know why, but anyway with my bearded dragons when they ran into a minor health situation like stuck nose shed or clocked femoral pores that did unfortunately require me to squeeze them out.... ( I know you should avoid doing this if possible ) but my male bearded dragon showed no signs of anger, but was uncomfortable obviously. He made no attempts at biting me or even hissing at me. He even still let me pick him up the next day. I really do believe he understood I was helping him, despite how uncomfortable he was, he still shows me signs of complete trust, and amazing thing is this is a bearded dragon I got as an Adult who clearly had 0 human interaction from previous owners as he wanted nothing to do with me, running away from me, puffing beard out at me and even hissing at me sometimes, it took time but he eventually realized I meant him no harm, and now has no issues with me holding him or petting him. I have pictures of before and after trust, but can't post links on Youtube. Animals really are smart, and will quickly gain trust in you, if you have the patience to work with them everyday and show them you mean them no harm.

People also tell me reptiles or in my case Bearded Dragons don't show emotions like a cat or dog does, and I couldn't disagree more, you can tell if they're happy or not. I looked up the signs of stress in bearded dragons and none of my beardys have ever showed signs of stress around me, in fact run to me when they do get scared. No joke decided to take my beardys out on a hot summer day within a few minutes a eagle was flying above my beardy ran to me instead of finding cover. ( I don't anyone to think I'm not looking up in the sky, I do almost every minute this bird must have been hiding in a tree when I brought them out )

video_description: I Don't Like Savannah Monitors as Pets #savannahmonitors #reptiles.
text_content: This hasn’t been my experience with savannah monitors at all. My family used to keep them when I was a kid. We had four in total and out of the four, three of them were dog tame. The sweetest, most soft and affectionate lizards you can imagine. The only one who was aggressive was a rescue from an abusive household and even she tamed down to a degree once she was in a safe environment and we’d put in enough time to build her trust. The other three were all so tame that I could handle them as a child. The only times I was ever bitten were during feeding misshapes when our first one was a baby (he’d miss the food and end up biting my fingers) and by our poor girl who came from that awful household I mentioned before. They were the only ones that bit and only one of them bit me on purpose. The one we raised from a hatchling would let go once he realised I wasn’t food and after going through that clumsy phase, I could actually hand feed him with no trouble at all. The other two never bit once.

video_title: What to know before owning a Savannah Monitor! 🦎
video_description: Meet this incredible #savannahmonitorlizard that visited our hospital for a wellness exam! Our #exoticpet doctor, Dr. Patt explains ...
text_content:
hi, doctor patt! i keep savannah monitors. they do not do well in an arid environment…in fact the reason they burrow is not only to escape predators but because the LOVE the moisture beneath the soil. they will develop severe shedding issues if kept in a dry habitat…i have seen this firsthand and am working on rehabilitating a monitor kept like that right now. savannah monitors also have a laterally (vertically) shaped tail which heavily implies swimming abilities…they love moisture, humidity, and water! my boy goes swimming by his own choice very often. moisture also helps their burrows keep their shape. it is very enriching for them, they are not like a bearded dragon. “Immad” made this same point. everything else in this video is accurate. other damaging misconceptions about them: they are insectivores, it is a common belief that they can be fed rats. this causes fatty liver disease, their number one killer.
people underestimate the temperatures that savannah monitors need…while they are usually kept at around 90-100 for their basking spots, they fare much better at 130 degrees and higher. 
people also still believe in infrared lights, which is damaging to their eyes and causes burns. i have seen this firsthand also. it is not effective and they do way better on full-spectrum lighting. 
most people also think that savannah monitors are easy, low-maintenance pets. this line of thinking kills savannah monitors! they are very expensive despite their initial price tag of $20-$40.

video_title: BABY Southern Alligator Lizard Care Guide | 2021
video_description: A comprehensive care guide on the Southern Alligator Lizard! Enjoy! - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - #alligatorlizard #careguide ...
text_content: Pretty good care info, but they do like humidity as they usually live near water.  Southern Alligator Lizards ( Elgaria )  are semi arboreal and like to climb. My baby is in a tall viv and is always up high basking and hunting. You did not mention UVB and they DO need it for bone health and health. They need a low UVB 5% or the new Arcadia arboreal shadedweller kit.  There are breeders and taking an adult from the wild is not such a great thing. They are aggressive and stress out and are probably carrying parasites and when stressed can make them sick. Always take a fecal sample to your reptile vet to make sure.  Babies are delicate but hardy and will take to being handled much better. Keep temps on the lower side even for the basking area. They can dehydrate easy especially babies.  I had one for 15 years and this is my second one. I also have its Mexican cousin ,  A Mexican Alligator lizard ( Abronia Graminae)  Bright green with yellow eyes and have had her for over a year.

video_title: I Recommend Pet Crocodiles FOR BEGINNERS! The Safest "Crocodilian" Pets!
video_description: Alligators and Crocodiles make TERRIBLE Pets for most reptile keepers right? These 5 Crocodilian pets make amazing pets even ...
text_content:
I have a crocodile gecko that I caught in my bedroom (they're common in my country, Portugal). They are very shy of human contact, specially if they're wild caught but they're so cute and cool looking. They're small and don't require a lot of care like other lizards, they're pretty hardy too. They just need a vertical terrarium cause they like to climb and also having caves/rocks/trunks on the floor so they can hide. Mine spends all day hidden inside a little cave and comes out at sunset/night. Sometimes she's out during the day but it's rare. They can drop their tail too and regrow it but it won't look as good as the original. From the experience I have with my crocodile gecko she won't drop her tail so easily, I've handled her while cleaning the terrarium and and when I moved her from terrarium and I could tell she was scared and stressed out cause of my presence and touch but she never dropped her tail which I think is a good sign. Even then, they'll often hide very quickly if they see people even from afar.
Ps: this is my experience with a wild caught and doesn't mean it applies to other geckos from the same species, specially if they're bred in captivity but I hope it helps. Also she was still a juvenile when caught and so far (2 years later), luckily, she's doing fine with no appearant issues.

video_title: Feeding my caiman !
text_content: Nigel and Cheesecake, along with their other pond friends, have lived together for a long time. You’ll notice many zoos, reptariums, aquariums etc. also house turtles and crocodilians/gators together, taking into consideration the size of the habitat and the species/behaviors of all animals involved. Since it is not ideal to keep more than one caiman in an enclosure and additional considerations need to be made in order to do so, quick turtles and large fish make ideal cohab candidates. Adding multiple species helps create an enriching environment, similar to their natural habitat. Without these animals, Nigel could potentially become depressed and (dare I say it for the new grads in the back,) even lonely. Although he snaps at Cheesecake’s overbearing and in-your-face behavior during snack time, it’s out of frustration, like “get out of the way damnit” rather than “I’m hungry and I want to eat you.” Nigel certainly could hurt Cheesecake during one of these interactions but it is highly unlikely as cheesecake is simply too fast for him and has years of “experience” dodging his annoyed snaps. Nigel lives with turtles, large slow fish and one little fish. All are healthy and happy. 👌

video_title: Buying Golden Mantella Females for my Breeding Project (Mantella Aurantiaca)
video_description: Join my trip to the local pet shop where they have managed to source some female Mantella for me after a long search.
text_content:
That would be good if you got them to be able to breed under your care. Apparently they're under tremendous pressures from over collection and rainforest destruction in Madagascar. I am not suggesting that your frogs were victims of an animal crime ring or anything, like importation of endangered animals illegally... no judgement. It doesn't matter. What matters is they went somewhere good - you! ✌🏽

I just happened to see a documentary; children from villages go "herping", albeit not as a westerner would, lol. They catch the frogs (of various species) for money, along with chameleons & geckos. And, if they can do it without the mother noticing, baby lemurs. The reptiles & amphibians are to be thrown haphazardly into the child's bag, and this is after being (fairly violently) pursued and captured.  Chameleons aren't carefully coerced out of treetops, onto a pole to grab, like they do in Florida; no, the kids just knock 'em to the ground. Hm. You'd think theyd be in trouble for this from the ringleader right? I'd think they would be more careful knowing that their meager income is involved. Lol. And the kids sometimes "casually" swing the bags around as they skip home. Nice. video_title: Top 5 WORST Pet Frogs and 5 BETTER Options You've Never Heard Of!
video_description: Frogs make great pets! But what are the worst frogs for beginners and the better options that make for easier captive frogs?
text_content:
I've got several of these animals:

I own a breeding pair of Vietnamese Mossy frogs (Theloderma corticale). I don't yet have amazon milk frogs, but they are on my list. I find the mossy frogs to be generally quite cryptic, they stay hidden most of the time unless stressed or feeding. They don't seem to rely on their camouflage that much, preferring to stay in the water and just cling to plants and wood. They do go through more gregarious periods, but generally, their flightiness takes over. That being said, I also now have over 20 tadpoles from them with more on the way. They are prolific breeders.

White's tree frogs are great, but best if you have a red night light to watch them hunt when they are awake. I've got three and they are fantastic pets that require almost no maintenance outside of feeding and scrubbing shed skin off of the glass. Bioactive enclosures make it so the poop just sort of disappears mostly.

Spanish ribbed newts are awesome too. I own 10 of them in my breeding colony, and they are by far the easiest aquatic animal I've ever owned. Extremely hardy, very robust, "playful", amorous and inquisitive, often coming to the glass of the tank to investigate me when I walk in the room. Probably expecting food, but not always as those same inquisitive newts will sometimes ignore the food in favour of staring at me and following me around.

I've got a pixie frog, and while I agree that feeding one is certainly a fun thing to do, I'll also add that they are the only amphibian I've encountered that you can actually build an owner-pet relationship. Something nearly on par with a cat or dog. Indeed I think if you can figure out the right incentive and strategy, they might even be smart enough to be trained to do simple tricks (trying to come up with a method now, just to see). Mine has proven to be an avid problem solver (gradually weakening the lid on his tub of a course of a couple months till the screen came loose so he could escape), and discerning eater, often conveying displeasure when I try to feed him food other than superworms. He knows not to bite me, but treats others as dangerous or hostile. He willingly comes and sits in my presence when I give him free roaming time (which I highly recommend, so long as the floor or environment is free of toxic chemicals). I try not to read into things too much, but I am almost certain that the inner life of a Pixie is a tad more complex than a lot of other amphibians.

video_description: Apparently the worlds deadliest frog makes a great pet!! So I tested it and it was awesome! if I used any sounds or videos that are ...
text_content:
Next time when feeding, you need to coat the bugs with calcium D3 powder to prevent Metabolic Bone Disease which is caused from calcium deficiency, or a lack of calcium. The calcium is important for the well being of the bones, nerve and muscle functions, and without it, can cause deformities, and even death.

Please do not mix dart frog species or morphs. All frogs must be the same morph and species to prevent territorialism and aggression which can lead to both frogs attacking, and potentially harming each other. Different morphs or species being housed together can also cause them hybridize which may mess up genes.

For the substrate, use natural substrate like coco fiber or reptisoil. The substrate in this video is just pebbles which can be used for a drainage layer at the bottom for water, but not good for a top layer.

While you did add in leaf litter, a bit of moss, such as spagma moss on the ground to maintain humidity in the vivarium would be a good idea.

Try not to add too much ventilation. Yes, a little bit of ventilation is good, but giving too much can cause both the enclosure, and the frogs to dry out. You can try covering up the top screen a bit, and leave a small amount of ventilation.

Please do not handle your frogs. Me saying this is not me caring too much about these animals, but me giving advice. Holding your dart frogs wont cause a bond, but will cause stress to the frog. These animals are skittish, and will flee when feeling threatened. Attempting to chase and grab it will only cause stress. All amphibians absorb liquids like water through their skin, and handling your frog can transfer oils, salt, and substances on your hand to the frog which can be harmful, so please try to refrain frog holding your dart frog from now on.

video_title: How Hunters Used Poison Dart Frogs 😲
text_content:
For more information:

The Emberá Chocó and Noanamá Chocó tribes, situated on the Pacific slopes of the Andes in Colombia, are known for using poison dart frogs to tip their blowgun darts. They specifically utilize three species of poison dart frogs: Phyllobates aurotaenia, Phyllobates bicolor, and Phyllobates terribilis. 

To collect the poison, the frogs are roasted over a fire. However, the steroids present in P. terribilis are so potent that it is sufficient to simply rub the dart on the back of the frog without killing it.

video_description: Choosing your first dart frog species can be a very difficult task, but today's video will hopefully help you narrow down your choice.
text_content:
Excellent video. I keep a bunch of the ones you listed, Amizonica being my first. I now have Oyapock, Azureus, Vanzolinii, Serensis, Fantastica monte cristo, Epipedobates anthonyi, and Opphaga pumilio punta laurel. The boldest are definitely the Oyapock and Azureus, but the Anthonyi are also incredibly bold. The fantastica is by far the least bold, often I will go 6 or 8 weeks without seeing her. the Serensis is similar but I hear him every morning. when I first got into frog keeping I bought frogs based on their beautiful coloration, but after a few years of keeping I started to buy them more based on their calls. the Oophaga pumilio probably has my favorite call, but the Vanzolinii, Serensis, and Leucamelas have incredible calls too. I wake up daily to them. The Anthonyi call more than all the other frogs combined and also have a very beautiful call. 

I would agree with everything you said here. You just make me wish I could have some phyllobates, unfortunately the entire genus is prohibited where I live for some insane reason I can't understand. 

Interestingly I keep my anthonyi in a very unconventional way. They share a very large paludarium with an emerald tree boa. I am very aware that darts can and readily do drown so a paludarium is not typically suggested but these ones seem to do well in there. The babies have never known any other environment having come out of the water in that tank. The tree boa is fully nocturnal and fully arboreal where the species of darts are quite terrestrial and diurnal. The tank is 48"x48"x18" or 122x122x45 cm with about 11 gallons of water which houses some neon tetras.

text_content:
For those confused.

Venomous animals inject their toxins, typically through bites or stings, while poisonous animals secrete or excrete toxins that are harmful when touched or ingested.
The most venomous is a Box Jellyfish

Edit for those saying the cone snail

Box jellyfish venom causes instant, severe pain and can lead to cardiac arrest and death within minutes, while a cone snail's venom causes paralysis over hours, potentially leading to respiratory and heart failure, but death is rarer and takes longer. The box jellyfish is considered the most venomous marine animal and is a direct threat to human life, whereas the geography cone snail's venom is also highly dangerous, but death is nearly exclusive to one species and is less common than from box jellyfish stings. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) considers The Box Jellyfish to be the most venomous marine animal in the world.

video_title: Rank These Animals To Have As A Pet! #shorts #animals #ranking #pet #tiger
text_content:
Remember, you gotta feed it and look after it. And there's the risk it can attack others (huge lawsuit/prison) or attack you instead (disabled/dead).

For those reasons:
1- Flying Squirrel (pretty cute, somewhat cool, not dangerous or expensive to look after)
2- Hermit Crab (even cheaper and easier to own)
3- Ostrich (okay, you will need a farm. Kinda dangerous but manageable)
4- Tiger (no way. You will basically become a zoo keeper)
5- Gorilla (cheaper and less dangerous than a Tiger, but note in many places Gorilla's have rights similar to Humans. So their care and habitat is to a much higher standard, meaning you have to be extremely wealthy to even consider this. Much more so than a Tiger).

video_title: How Raccoons (Almost) Got Away With It
video_description: Nessie's Linktree- https://linktr.ee/Lochnessofficial TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@lochnessofficial?_r=1&_t=ZT-93hL3Ntn1mY ...
text_content:
EDITED TO ADD
I wasn’t expecting so many reactions, so could you do me a favor and read through the thread before asking questions or making comments to see if yours has already been answered? I’m repeating myself a lot. 

Exotic mammals veterinarian here. I treated pet and wild raccoons for three decades and I’m here to tell you the babies are super-cute and friendly. BUT…once they reach adulthood, most of them just want you dead. I saw more owners get bitten by raccoons than any other pet. You have to sedate them to do literally anything to them, even brush them.

They’re smart, they’re cute, they’re funny—all those things—as long as they get to do anything they want. If you try to stop them from eating your kitchen cabinets or shredding your mattress, it’s over. Not to mention that yes, they wash their food in bowls. But, what most people don’t know is that they also poop in those bowls. 

They make terrible pets. Enjoy them in the wild and leave them alone.

I’ll also add this: 

Raccoons are one of the Big Four rabies vector species. In most of Europe, they’re considered such a dangerous invasive species that they can be hunted, trapped, or shot on sight. In most U.S. states, you need a special permit to own one. 

They also have a high incidence of what’s known as the “raccoon roundworm,” which can be transmitted to dogs and people. In people, it invades the brain and can cause all kinds of neurological symptoms, including death. Children are at most risk, because transmission is from fecal contamination of soil, i.e., putting dirty fingers in mouths. 

Fortunately, human deaths from raccoon rabies or roundworm are rare, but please don’t push it by trying to make one a pet.

text_content: Raccoons are scary smart, and I'm convinced theyre smarter than we realize. I used to work nights, so Id be up at all hours even on my days off. I once had a some late-night movie watching interrupted by what sounded like a cat trying to imitate whalesong coming from my carport. I poked my head out a window to see about a dozen raccoons of various sizes *sitting* (on their haunches) *in a perfect circle* while one large raccoon stood i  the center of the circle,  "talking" and gesticulating wildly. This went on for minutes, then suddenly the "talking" one dropped to all fours, quickly followed by the rest, and then they instantly scattered into the darkness. At the time I lived two hours south of Toronto, so same stock as the UberCoons of t-dot

video_description: He Lost His Home for Being Too Loud—Now This Cockatoo Fills One With Song ❤️ He was surrendered at just four years old ...
text_content: That's how I got my cocktail. He was to loud for my sister and due to her health it got on her nerves. I loved him anyway. I bought my mom a Quaker parrot and he refused to speak in front of me. He eventually slipped up and I finally heard him talk because he didn't know I was in the house. I laughed and I watched him stretch his neck trying to see where I was at. He immediately shut up and didn't say anything until I left the room. He used to go for my feet trying to bite me through my shoes. I asked the woman I got him from about his home life. She had another bird that was a female cockatial so I told my mom he hated me because I took him away from his girlfriend. Such good times. He finally passed and since it was winter and the ground was frozen I wrapped him in a towel, put him in a ziplock bag and placed him in the freezer until we could give him a proper burial. I always wanted another bird but with my two cats I think it would traumatize the bird. I just enjoy my wild birds.

text_content: Sanctuaries are filled with cockatoos. They look fun and snuggly in videos, but they can be unpredictable, aggressive, and LOUD, and I mean hearing loss loud. If there's a falcon within a half mile of my house, everyone within a 2 mile radius will know about it. And that beak is no joke, it can bite through blocks of wood AND your face. So many people don't understand this prior to getting one. That said, they are amazing characters if you commit to changing your life to accommodate them, which includes living with the noise. I love that this woman was volunteering at a parrot rescue when she met this boy. What a great story! And a fantastic way to explore what these birds are really like, and set them up for success.

text_content:
My U2 is not sure what to make of the sounds coming from my iPad right now 😂

My baby came to us back in 2014. We were his 3rd home in 4yrs…because he is loud 😢. He was sooooo afraid of balls because they had been thrown at his cage when he screamed. Afraid of anything moving too fast around him because someone hit his cage….for being a cockatoo and being loud. We love him so much and I cannot imaging having a quiet home…and can never live in an apartment 😂😂😂😂. My oldest…his true human,  will take him when I am too old to care for him. 💖

I am single in large part because when someone walks into my home and my birds (I also have an Amazon) start talking to you and you don’t respond….yeah, that tells me all I need to know. 🤷‍♀️ Sydney has been with my family longer than anyone else in his life and I feel so blessed that he loves us.

video_description: Grab your emotional support rock. Adjust your scrunchie. Try not to change the channel during the infomercial. In this extended ...
text_content:
Thank you for the generosity of the time and talent that you devoted to this dissection and analysis. I have listened attentively to the podcast, and am enjoying for the second time on YouTube.  My only extra contribution to this masterful summary would be about the care with which Taylor has chosen the metaphors for the exes. Both a rock and cactus have mixed attributes, unlike the garbage that no-one healthy wants to keep around. A rock is how we refer to someone who has been steady and dependable when we’re in times of trouble. They meet immediate needs and they’re so reassuring when we aren’t feeling equipped to deal with present circumstances. Taylor went through a time when a rock was a comfort and a big, safe support. But then she found her feet and her own intrinsic strength again, and her rock couldn’t morph into a more flexible companion, who could meet her emerging and more nuanced needs. But it probably remained a very nice rock. A cactus also has many positive attributes. For instance, they’re often very attractive, and even the dull ones often have gorgeous blooms that suddenly emerge and amaze everyone. They have stores of vital supplies of life-giving water, that can sustain creatures with the skills or correct body types to get past the spikes to reach to that water.  But they aren’t nurturing or sustaining for everyone.  Not everyone can get past those spikes. 
If Taylor wants to make a music video for every song on this album, I will be a very happy fan.

video_description: A classroom pet gets a makeover. This eastern box turtle has been in captivity legally for over a decade, but a lack of consistent ...
text_content:
Lovely wee guy! Thank you for working so well on him. He looks much happier now!!

I'm always amazed at captured turtles that do well in captivity from thw wild. About 25ish years ago, an ex and I came upon 2 baby red-eared sliders in 2 different spots about 6 weeks apart when fishing. One had just lost an eye- it was a raw mess, and one just lost a leg. We got them home and I had an old tank that was empty so to the petstore we went for supplies, got them seen at a vet, and got their wounds cleaned. They were healing and doing ok, eating and drinking and pooping. However around 2 weeks in, both began flipping out. The one missing an eye would smack himself into the same corner repeatedly. The other one began something similar but not as violent. I have no idea why, the vet also had no idea why but just said that although we gave them a large tank with water and good substrate, that they still were likely unhappy being confined and recommended us to let them go. We brought them both to a quieter pond that didn't have large fish or snapping turtles and let them go. I always wondered 1. if they survived to adulthood with one minus an eye and one minus a limb and 2. If so, would they find each other and live together somehow. I never set out to bring home baby turtles, I couldn't just leave them like that. I hope they made it.

video_title: My little guyyy #chronicillness #health #reptile #pet #snake
text_content: you can tell the gender by the thickness of the tail! after the vent (the hole) on your snakes belly if the width is the same as the body and slowly tapers its a female, if it turns skinny fast (smaller than the width of the snake) it is male :] this can be hard to tell sometimes also depending on age so the other methods are probing and popping out hemipeens (if there are any, its a male) but those two methods are better adviced to trained professionals and vets as it is a delicate process and can cause injury or prolapse which is not good for our noodle friends! hope this helped :]

text_content:
Five Nights at Freddy's (FNaF) is a massive and intricate horror game franchise known for its deep, often cryptic lore. It primarily revolves around a fictional restaurant chain called Freddy Fazbear's Pizza, reminiscent of Chuck E. Cheese, where animatronic characters entertain children by day and become terrifying, haunted threats by night.
​Here's a breakdown of the core elements of the FNaF lore:
​1. The Missing Children Incident and the Killer:
​William Afton: The primary antagonist and co-founder of Fazbear Entertainment. He is a serial killer who, motivated by a dark obsession with immortality and remnant (the supernatural energy left behind by tragic events), murders several children.
​The Missing Children Incident: This refers to the initial murders where William, often disguised as the animatronic Spring Bonnie, lures and kills at least five children at Freddy Fazbear's Pizza.
​Possession: The spirits of these murdered children possess the animatronics:
​Freddy Fazbear
​Bonnie the Bunny
​Chica the Chicken
​Foxy the Pirate Fox
​Golden Freddy (or Fredbear), often believed to be possessed by the spirit of his first victim, Cassidy, or a combination of spirits.
​2. The Afton Family Tragedy:
​Elizabeth Afton: William's daughter, who is killed by his creation, Circus Baby, at Circus Baby's Entertainment and Rental (a sister location). Her spirit possesses Baby.
​The Crying Child (Evan Afton/BV): William's younger son, who is terrified of the animatronics. He dies from a bite inflicted by Fredbear (the original Golden Freddy animatronic) during a birthday party prank orchestrated by his older brother, Michael. This is known as "The Bite of '83." His spirit is often theorized to be Golden Freddy, or part of Golden Freddy, or even Puppet.
​Michael Afton: William's older son. He attempts to atone for his father's sins and his own role in his brother's death. He works at various Fazbear locations, including the first Freddy's, Circus Baby's Entertainment and Rental (where he is scooped and becomes a walking corpse inhabited by Ennard), and eventually becomes the night guard in later games, trying to free the trapped souls. He is a significant protagonist.
​3. Remnant and Immortality:
​William Afton discovers "remnant," a supernatural metallic substance created by agony and traumatic death. He learns that by infusing animatronics with remnant, he can imbue them with consciousness and effectively make the souls "immortal" within the machines.
​His experiments with remnant and his desire to understand and control it are key to his villainy and the prolonged suffering of the children's souls.
​4. Key Locations and Events:
​Freddy Fazbear's Pizza (Various Incarnations): The primary setting for many of the games, where the haunted animatronics lurk.
​Circus Baby's Entertainment and Rental: A more advanced animatronic facility designed by William Afton, featuring "funtime" animatronics designed to capture children.
​Fazbear's Fright: The Horror Attraction: A haunted attraction built around the urban legends of Freddy Fazbear's Pizza, where Springtrap (William Afton trapped in the Spring Bonnie suit) is discovered.
​Freddy Fazbear's Pizzeria Simulator (FNaF 6): Michael Afton, guided by Henry Emily, creates a new pizzeria as a trap to lure and burn all remaining animatronics containing remnant (Springtrap, Molten Freddy, Scrap Baby, Lefty) to finally free the souls.
​Ultimate Custom Night: Often interpreted as William Afton's personal hell, tormented by the animatronics he created and the souls he wronged.
​Help Wanted: A VR game within the FNaF universe, where a digital virus (Glitchtrap, an AI manifestation of William Afton's consciousness) tries to escape into the real world.
​Security Breach: Set in the massive Mega Pizzaplex, featuring new animatronics and the return of William Afton (as Burntrap) and other older threats.
​5. Henry Emily:
​William Afton's business partner and co-founder of Fazbear Entertainment.
​He is the brilliant engineer behind many of the animatronics.
​His daughter, Charlotte (Charlie), is William's first victim, her spirit possessing The Puppet.
​Henry becomes the force for good, dedicating his life to ending William's terror and freeing the souls, culminating in the "FNaF 6" fire.
​6. The Puppet (Marionette):
​A highly significant animatronic, often seen as a protector.
​Possessed by Henry's daughter, Charlotte.
​It's believed to be the one who gave life to the other animatronics by placing the spirits of the murdered children into them.
​7. Springtrap/Scraptrap/Burntrap:
​William Afton's ultimate fate: after dismantling the original animatronics and releasing the spirits, he is cornered by the freed souls and crushed inside the Spring Bonnie suit, becoming Springtrap.
​He survives fires and appears in multiple games, continuously trying to return.
​Summary of the Narrative Arc:
​The FNaF lore tells a tragic story of a family torn apart by a monstrous father, the enduring suffering of murdered children, and the desperate attempts of others (Michael, Henry) to bring an end to the cycle of violence and free the tormented souls. It's a complex puzzle of unreliable narrators, hidden clues, and evolving mysteries that has kept fans speculating for years.
